solver_view.stem: A cylindrical lithium-ion battery undergoes thermal runaway triggered by external heating. The internal temperature is monitored via an embedded thermocouple. Engineers observe that switching from 150 W to 300 W heating power does NOT produce a proportionally higher peak internal temperature. Which of the following statements correctly explain this observation? Select all that apply.
options: A. Internal peak temperature during thermal runaway is determined jointly by external heating power and internal exothermic chain reactions, so higher external power does not guarantee higher internal temperature.
B. At higher heating power, the pressure relief valve opens earlier, venting more energy and systematically capping internal temperature below that of lower-power tests.
C. Internal combustion of electrode materials and electrolyte contributes independently to peak temperature, meaning the internal reaction magnitude can dominate over external power input.
D. The embedded thermocouple introduces sufficient resistance change to suppress internal temperature readings at high power, making higher temperatures appear artificially lower.
E. Because all tested peak internal temperatures remained below 1100°C regardless of power level, the internal chain reaction energy release has a practical upper bound in these cells.
F. Higher heating power always produces faster temperature rise rates, which necessarily results in higher peak temperatures due to reduced heat dissipation time.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly supported: internal temperature is influenced by both external heating and internal chain reactions, not external power alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Valve venting timing is not evidenced to systematically cap peak internal temperature; this is a mechanism substitution overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported: material combustion characteristics within the battery independently influence internal temperature beyond external power.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermocouple implantation caused small resistance changes (~3%) but is not evidenced to suppress temperature readings at high power.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported: all recorded peak internal temperatures remained below 1100°C, consistent with a practical energy ceiling from internal reactions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reverses the evidenced finding; higher power does not necessarily yield higher peak temperature, contradicting C009 directly.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Understanding that peak internal temperature during thermal runaway is not monotonically controlled by external heating power due to internal chain reaction contributions.
analysis.primary_boundary: The non-monotonic relationship between heating power and peak internal temperature is conditional on the presence of internal exothermic reactions and material combustion; it does not generalize to all heating scenarios.
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming valve venting alone caps internal temperature
Attributing temperature suppression to thermocouple resistance effects

solver_view.stem: A battery safety engineer is comparing thermal runaway behavior between a fresh cell and an aged cell (SOH = 0.8) of the same chemistry and format, both tested under identical 300 W external heating. The aged cell exhibited explosion and fragmented into two pieces; the fresh cell did not. The aged cell's pressure relief valve also opened at a lower temperature than the fresh cell. Which statements are supported by these observations? Select all that apply.
options: A. The aged cell's lower valve opening temperature indicates earlier internal pressure buildup, consistent with more reactive internal conditions at the same heating power.
B. The explosion in the aged cell confirms that aging universally increases thermal runaway severity across all heating powers and all SOC levels.
C. The aged cell exhibited significantly higher maximum external temperatures than the fresh cell, indicating a more intensified thermal runaway reaction.
D. The lower valve opening temperature of the aged cell is caused solely by mechanical degradation of the valve seal rather than internal chemical changes.
E. Because the fresh cell did not explode at 300 W, it cannot undergo thermal runaway fragmentation under any heating condition.
F. The combination of lower valve opening temperature and explosion in the aged cell suggests aging alters both the pressure response threshold and the severity of the runaway event.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower valve opening temperature in aged cell is consistent with earlier pressure buildup, supported by C005 and C008.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Explosion was observed only at 300 W for SOH=0.8; generalizing to all powers and SOC levels is an unsupported boundary overreach.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly supported by C014: aged batteries showed significantly higher external temperatures, indicating more intense thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Attributing lower valve temperature solely to mechanical seal degradation is a mechanism substitution; evidence does not isolate this cause.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Absence of fragmentation at one power level cannot exclude fragmentation under all conditions; this is an inadmissible universal negative.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported by C005 and C008 together: aging shifts both the valve opening threshold and the runaway severity outcome.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing evidenced aging effects on valve threshold and runaway severity from unsupported universal generalizations about aging.
analysis.primary_boundary: Aging-related explosion was observed only at 300 W with SOH=0.8; results cannot be extended to all powers, all SOC levels, or attributed to a single isolated mechanism.
disallowed_extrapolations: Universal severity increase across all conditions
Sole mechanical explanation for valve threshold shift

solver_view.stem: A two-level thermal runaway early warning system is designed for a lithium-ion battery pack using external surface temperature sensors. Level-1 warning triggers at an external surface temperature of 82°C (corresponding to internal SEI decomposition at ~80°C). Level-2 warning triggers at 92°C external (corresponding to internal temperature rise rate acceleration at ~100°C before valve opening). A gas concentration sensor is also installed. Which of the following conclusions about this warning system are correct?
options: A. The Level-1 threshold at 82°C external is set to correspond to the onset of SEI decomposition internally, providing warning before the pressure relief valve opens.
B. The gas concentration sensor can provide earlier warning than the temperature-based Level-1 threshold because gas leaks begin before SEI decomposition.
C. The Level-2 threshold at 92°C external corresponds to an internal condition where temperature rise rate is accelerating but the pressure relief valve has not yet opened.
D. Gas concentration monitoring alone is insufficient as a primary early warning method because detectable gas concentration only appears after the pressure relief valve has already loosened.
E. Because the pressure relief valve of a 100% SOC battery opens between 130°C and 150°C externally, both warning levels are triggered well before valve opening at full charge.
F. The two-level system guarantees prevention of thermal runaway if operators respond within standard response times, since both thresholds precede the runaway onset.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly supported by C012: external 82°C maps to internal SEI decomposition at 80°C, preceding valve opening.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas detection only occurs after valve loosening per C010, making it later than temperature-based Level-1; direction is reversed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported by C013: Level-2 at 92°C external corresponds to internal acceleration point at 100°C, before valve opens.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly supported by C010: detectable gas concentration only appears post-valve loosening, meaning thermal runaway has already begun.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C002 gives valve opening at 130–150°C as internal temperature for 100% SOC, not external; applying it as external temperature is a scale transfer error.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence establishes warning thresholds but does not support a guarantee of prevention; this is an unsupported causal overclaim about system outcome.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Evaluating the admissibility of warning threshold assignments and the limitations of gas-based versus temperature-based detection strategies.
analysis.primary_boundary: Warning thresholds are defined for specific internal-external temperature mappings; gas detection is explicitly post-valve, not pre-runaway; prevention guarantee is unsupported.
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating internal valve opening temperature as external surface temperature
Claiming gas sensors provide earlier warning than SEI-based thresholds

solver_view.prompt: During thermal runaway experiments on lithium-ion batteries at 0% SOC, the maximum internal temperature ranged from 480°C to 1020°C across repeated trials, while at 50% SOC the internal temperature reached approximately 1017.8°C with a maximum rise rate of 297.3°C/s. At 100% SOC, the maximum internal-to-external temperature difference reached 429.8°C, and the highest recorded internal temperature across all tests was 1063.2°C. An engineer claims that because 0% SOC batteries can reach internal temperatures up to 1020°C, which overlaps with 50% SOC peak values, SOC level has no meaningful effect on thermal runaway intensity. Evaluate this claim, identifying what the evidence actually supports and what reasoning error the engineer is making.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's claim is not supported. The evidence establishes a SOC-dependent threshold: at SOC ≥ 50%, thermal runaway is more intense and internal temperature exceeds 900°C as a consistent outcome. While the 0% SOC upper bound of 1020°C numerically overlaps with the 50% SOC peak of ~1017.8°C, the 0% SOC range spans 480°C to 1020°C, indicating high variability and that lower temperatures are also possible at 0% SOC. The engineer commits a boundary overreach error by using the upper tail of the 0% SOC distribution to dismiss the SOC effect, ignoring that the lower bound at 0% SOC (480°C) is far below the ≥900°C threshold consistently associated with SOC ≥ 50%. The evidence supports SOC as a meaningful intensity modifier, not a negligible variable.
must_include: SOC ≥ 50% threshold for intensified runaway and >900°C internal temperature
0% SOC range is 480–1020°C showing high variability, not a fixed high value
Overlap of upper bounds does not negate the SOC effect on the lower bound and consistency of outcomes
The engineer's error is using the upper tail of the 0% SOC distribution to dismiss a threshold-based SOC effect
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming SOC has no effect on thermal runaway intensity
Treating the 0% SOC maximum as representative of typical 0% SOC behavior
Concluding that all SOC levels produce equivalent runaway severity based on overlapping peak values

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing between distributional overlap of extreme values and the evidenced threshold-based SOC effect on thermal runaway intensity.
analysis.primary_boundary: The SOC ≥ 50% threshold for >900°C internal temperature is a consistent finding; the 0% SOC range includes much lower temperatures, making upper-tail overlap an invalid basis for dismissing the SOC effect.

solver_view.stem: A 300 Ah LFP cell undergoes thermal runaway testing under two conditions: (A) with flaming combustion present and (B) without ignition. Both tests are conducted at 100% SOC. An engineer reviews the results and makes four claims about the relationship between ignition state and thermal runaway outcomes. Which of the following claims are CORRECTLY supported by the experimental evidence?
options: A. Flaming combustion accelerates the timing of thermal runaway onset in the single cell.
B. Flaming combustion significantly elevates the peak surface temperature above the value reached without ignition.
C. Peak surface temperature is primarily governed by internal heat generation rather than external flame contribution.
D. Mass loss after thermal runaway is greater in the ignited cell than in the non-ignited cell at the same SOC.
E. The cell surface can exceed 600°C without self-igniting, confirming LFP's relative thermal stability under external heating.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states flaming combustion accelerates TR occurrence in a single cell.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states peak surface temperature is less influenced by flaming combustion; reverses the reported trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states peak surface temperature is mainly determined by internal heat generation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states no strict relationship exists between mass loss and ignition occurrence at the same SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence confirms the 300 Ah LFP cell did not self-ignite even at surface temperatures exceeding 600°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing the effect of flaming combustion on TR timing versus peak temperature versus mass loss in LFP cells
analysis.primary_boundary: Ignition affects TR onset timing but not peak surface temperature or mass loss at fixed SOC
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing ignition-driven temperature elevation to all chemistries
Inferring mass loss is ignition-dependent across all SOC levels

solver_view.stem: A 300 Ah LFP cell pack is stored in an 80 m³ enclosed garage. Ten cells undergo thermal runaway simultaneously at 100% SOC without flaming combustion. The garage ventilation system provides a fresh air renewal rate of 4 h⁻¹. An engineer assesses CO toxicity risk and ventilation adequacy. Which of the following statements are INCORRECTLY reasoned given these conditions?
options: A. A renewal rate of 4 h⁻¹ is sufficient to keep CO concentration below 667 ppm for 100% SOC cells without combustion.
B. The fractional effective dose (XFED) at 4 h⁻¹ renewal rate exceeds the critical threshold of 1, indicating serious toxicity risk.
C. To maintain CO below 667 ppm for 100% SOC non-combusting cells, the renewal rate must reach at least 107 h⁻¹.
D. At 4 h⁻¹ renewal, CO toxicity risk is negligible because LFP cells without ignition generate minimal CO.
E. The 100% SOC cell without combustion generates approximately 42.8 g of CO from internal battery reactions, making the 4 h⁻¹ rate inadequate for safe CO dilution.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (wrong claim)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states 107 h⁻¹ is the minimum required; 4 h⁻¹ is far below this threshold and therefore insufficient.

option_judgments.label: Correct (wrong claim)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence confirms XFED greatly exceeds 1 at 4 h⁻¹, indicating catastrophic toxicity; this statement is correctly reasoned.

option_judgments.label: Correct (wrong claim)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states 107 h⁻¹ is the minimum renewal rate to keep CO below 667 ppm; correctly reasoned.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (wrong claim)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows 100% SOC non-combusting cells generate 42.8 g CO; dismissing risk at 4 h⁻¹ is unsupported and incorrect.

option_judgments.label: Correct (wrong claim)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence confirms 42.8 g CO from internal reactions at 100% SOC without combustion, supporting inadequacy of 4 h⁻¹.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying incorrectly reasoned ventilation and CO toxicity claims under defined garage and SOC conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: 107 h⁻¹ minimum renewal rate required; 4 h⁻¹ is grossly insufficient; XFED >> 1 at 4 h⁻¹
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming CO generation is negligible without combustion
Extending 20% SOC CO values to 100% SOC conditions

solver_view.stem: A battery engineer compares peak heat release rate (HRR) and total heat release (THR) across three 300 Ah LFP cells tested at 100%, 50%, and 20% SOC under flaming combustion. The engineer proposes four design-relevant conclusions. Which conclusions are CORRECTLY supported by the experimental data?
options: A. Peak HRR decreases disproportionately between 100% and 50% SOC compared to the decrease between 50% and 20% SOC.
B. THR values are nearly identical across all three SOC levels, implying SOC has negligible influence on total combustion energy.
C. The dominant driver of THR is the quantity of flammable cell components, not the electrochemical state of charge.
D. Because THR is similar across SOC levels, peak HRR is also similar across SOC levels.
E. The peak HRR of a 100% SOC cell is hazard-equivalent to a methanol pool fire with a diameter of approximately 0.61 m.
F. Reducing SOC from 100% to 50% is a more effective strategy for limiting peak fire intensity than reducing from 50% to 20%.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states peak HRR drops sharply from 88.78 kW to 16.73 kW (100%→50%) versus only ~4 kW difference (50%→20%).

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: THR values differ (16238, 16138, 13568 kJ); 20% SOC is notably lower, so SOC is not entirely negligible for THR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states combustion heat is mainly determined by flammable component quantity, not SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Similar THR does not imply similar peak HRR; evidence shows large peak HRR differences despite similar THR values.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states 100% SOC peak HRR is equivalent to a methanol pool fire of 0.61 m diameter.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The disproportionate HRR drop from 100% to 50% SOC versus 50% to 20% supports greater fire intensity reduction in the upper SOC range.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing SOC effects on peak HRR versus THR and inferring design-relevant fire mitigation strategies
analysis.primary_boundary: Peak HRR is strongly SOC-dependent; THR is primarily driven by flammable component quantity; the two metrics are decoupled
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming similar THR implies similar peak HRR
Generalizing LFP HRR equivalence to other cell chemistries or sizes

solver_view.prompt: A first responder extinguishes the visible electrolyte flame on a 300 Ah LFP cell that has been externally heated but does not apply any cooling to the battery body afterward. The responder believes the hazard is eliminated because the flame is out. Using the evidence from large-format LFP thermal runaway testing, explain why this assessment is incorrect, identify what additional hazard remains, and describe the mechanism by which the local temperature distribution inside the cell—specifically the role of electrolyte distribution—affects the thermal progression observed during thermal runaway.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The responder's assessment is incorrect because extinguishing the electrolyte flame without cooling the battery body does not terminate the thermal runaway process. Evidence shows that if the flame is extinguished but the cell is not cooled, the battery can still enter thermal runaway with a more aggressive release of flammable gases and heat than before, creating a heightened rather than reduced hazard. The remaining hazard is internally driven thermal runaway from exothermic electrochemical decomposition reactions that continue independently of external flame. Regarding internal temperature distribution: when the safety valve opens, electrolyte boiling absorbs heat locally, causing temperature decreases at thermocouple sites. The magnitude of this cooling effect at each location depends on local electrolyte concentration—sites with more electrolyte nearby experience larger temperature drops due to greater latent heat absorption during boiling. This means the thermal front propagates non-uniformly through the cell, a spatial effect most observable in large-format cells where front-to-back TR propagation is distinct.
must_include: Extinguishing flame without cooling does not stop thermal runaway
Remaining hazard: more aggressive flammable gas and heat release after flame extinction
Internal reactions drive TR independently of external flame
Electrolyte boiling at safety valve opening causes local temperature decrease
Greater local electrolyte concentration produces larger local cooling effect
TR spatial propagation is observable in large-format cells but may not be in smaller samples
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming flame extinction always triggers more aggressive TR in all LIB chemistries
Asserting electrolyte distribution effect has been quantified for cells other than this 300 Ah LFP format
Inferring that cooling the flame side alone is sufficient to prevent TR propagation to the back surface

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-mechanism hazard reasoning: flame extinction fallacy, internally driven TR continuation, and electrolyte-distribution-dependent local thermal effects
analysis.primary_boundary: Evidence is specific to 300 Ah LFP large-format cells; flame extinction without body cooling increases rather than eliminates residual hazard
intended_error_modes: Assuming flame extinction terminates all TR hazard (causal overclaim)
Attributing local cooling effect to external rather than internal electrolyte boiling (mechanism substitution)
Generalizing large-cell TR spatial propagation to small test samples (scale transfer overclaim)

solver_view.stem: A 2×2 array of 21700 lithium-ion cells undergoes thermal runaway in one cell. Water spray is applied at 0.2 MPa. Tests show that 0.7 L at 0.1 MPa prevents propagation, but increasing pressure to 0.2 MPa requires 1.05 L to achieve the same outcome. Which of the following statements are consistent with these observations? Select all that apply.
options: A. Higher water pressure reduces contact efficiency with the batteries, requiring greater total water volume to compensate for reduced continuous cooling.
B. Higher water pressure inherently delivers more cooling energy per unit volume, so less total water is needed at 0.2 MPa than at 0.1 MPa.
C. Insufficient water volume at any pressure may delay thermal runaway propagation onset without preventing it.
D. The cooling rate after water spray stops is significantly faster at higher pressure due to improved heat transfer.
E. Preventing propagation requires meeting a threshold defined by both pressure and total water volume, not pressure alone.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher pressure reduces spray duration and contact efficiency, requiring more total water volume to prevent propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows more water is needed at higher pressure, contradicting the claim that higher pressure reduces required volume.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states insufficient volume or low contact efficiency delays but does not prevent propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states no significant difference in cooling rate after spray stops across tested conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Propagation prevention thresholds differ by pressure condition, confirming both variables jointly determine outcome.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Joint pressure-volume threshold reasoning for thermal runaway propagation prevention
analysis.primary_boundary: Prevention requires sufficient total water volume AND adequate contact efficiency; pressure alone does not determine outcome
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing cooling rate differences to high-pressure conditions not tested
Inferring higher pressure always improves cooling efficiency

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway of a 21700 LIB containing LiPF6 electrolyte, water spray is applied to the burning cell. Compared to combustion without water spray, measurements show CO and H2 concentrations increase, CO2 decreases, and HF concentration increases. Which of the following correctly identify both a correct trend AND its mechanistic cause?
options: A. HF increases because water reacts with PF5, a LiPF6 decomposition product, generating HF and phosphoryl fluoride.
B. CO2 increases because water-gas shift reaction converts CO to CO2 in the presence of excess steam.
C. CO increases because water spray promotes incomplete combustion by reducing local flame temperature and oxygen availability.
D. HF increases because fluorine is released directly from cathode oxide decomposition accelerated by water contact.
E. H2 increases because steam reforming reactions between water and hydrocarbon combustion intermediates produce hydrogen gas.
F. CO2 decreases, consistent with water spray suppressing complete oxidation pathways in the combustion zone.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states water reacts with PF5 from LiPF6 decomposition to produce HF and PF3O, explaining HF increase.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows CO2 decreases with water spray; water-gas shift increasing CO2 contradicts the reported trend.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO increase trend is correct but the mechanism is unsupported speculation; evidence does not attribute it to flame temperature reduction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes HF increase to PF5 hydrolysis from electrolyte, not cathode oxide decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: H2 increase trend is correct but steam reforming mechanism is not supported by the evidence bundle.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CO2 decrease with water spray is directly reported; this option correctly states the trend without overclaiming mechanism.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing evidence-supported trend-mechanism pairs from plausible but unsupported causal attributions
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism must be explicitly supported; correct trend paired with wrong mechanism is inadmissible
disallowed_extrapolations: Attributing H2 or CO changes to specific unsupported reaction pathways
Inferring water-gas shift or steam reforming from trend data alone

solver_view.stem: An engineer designing a water-spray suppression system for a lithium-ion battery energy storage module references test data from 21700 cells in a 2×2 configuration. The recommended parameters are 207 L min⁻¹ m⁻² spray intensity, 20.8 g W⁻¹h⁻¹ water quantity, at 0.1 MPa. Which conclusions are NOT warranted by the evidence? Select all that apply.
options: A. These parameters are sufficient to prevent thermal runaway propagation in all multi-cell LIB configurations regardless of cell chemistry or format.
B. Applying these parameters at 0.2 MPa instead of 0.1 MPa will maintain equivalent or better suppression with less total water.
C. These parameters represent a validated threshold for the tested 2×2 21700 configuration at 0.1 MPa.
D. Increasing spray pressure above 0.1 MPa may reduce contact efficiency and require more water volume to achieve the same outcome.
E. These parameters can be directly scaled to large-format pouch or prismatic cells without further validation.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT warranted)
option_judgments.justification: Parameters were derived from a specific 2×2 21700 configuration; universalizing to all chemistries and formats is an unsupported scale transfer overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT warranted)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows higher pressure reduces contact efficiency and requires more water; claiming equivalence or improvement at 0.2 MPa with less water contradicts evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS warranted)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly supports these parameters as effective thresholds for the tested configuration at 0.1 MPa.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS warranted)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states higher pressure reduces contact efficiency and that more water was needed at 0.2 MPa.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT warranted)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence is limited to 21700 cylindrical cells; direct scaling to other formats is an unsupported regime transfer extrapolation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility judgment on generalization of suppression parameters beyond tested scope
analysis.primary_boundary: Parameters validated only for 2×2 21700 configuration at 0.1 MPa; format, chemistry, and pressure generalization are unsupported
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying 21700 thresholds to pouch or prismatic cells
Assuming higher pressure maintains or improves suppression efficiency

solver_view.prompt: In a water-spray suppression test on burning 21700 lithium-ion batteries containing LiPF6 electrolyte, applying water increases HF concentration compared to combustion without water. Explain the two-step causal chain that produces this outcome, identify which electrolyte component is the origin, and state why this finding means water spray cannot be evaluated solely on thermal suppression performance when assessing overall safety.

ground_truth.gold_answer: LiPF6 thermally decomposes during battery combustion, producing PF5 as an intermediate decomposition product. When water spray contacts the burning cell, water reacts chemically with PF5 to generate HF and phosphoryl fluoride (PF3O). This two-step chain—LiPF6 decomposition yielding PF5, then PF5 hydrolysis yielding HF—means water spray simultaneously suppresses thermal hazard while generating increased toxic gas hazard. Therefore, evaluating water spray solely on temperature reduction or propagation prevention is insufficient; the toxicological impact of elevated HF emissions must also be assessed to characterize overall suppression safety.
must_include: LiPF6 as the electrolyte source
PF5 as the intermediate decomposition product
water reacting with PF5 to produce HF
HF concentration increases with water application
thermal suppression benefit does not eliminate toxic gas hazard
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming HF originates from cathode material decomposition
Asserting water spray universally worsens safety outcomes
Quantifying HF concentrations beyond what the stem provides
Attributing HF generation to direct water-electrolyte solvent reaction without PF5 pathway

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain from electrolyte decomposition to toxic gas generation under water spray, and its implication for safety assessment scope
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism is specific to LiPF6-containing cells; HF increase is caused by PF5 hydrolysis, not direct fluoride release from other sources

solver_view.stem: A LiCoO2/graphite pouch cell at 95% SOC undergoes mechanical indentation. Temperature rises progressively and reaches 150°C. Which of the following statements correctly characterize the thermal runaway sequence and heat-generation roles of the four exothermic reactions identified for this chemistry and loading mode?
options: A. SEI decomposition initiates at 130°C and is the first reaction to occur, preceding the anode-electrolyte reaction.
B. The cathode-electrolyte reaction, occurring near 184°C, contributes the most cumulative heat and is therefore the key trigger for violent thermal runaway.
C. The anode-electrolyte reaction at 150°C is the key contributor to total heat generation and determines the onset of violent thermal runaway.
D. Electrolyte decomposition at approximately 230°C exhibits the highest exothermic peak among the four reactions.
E. The cathode-electrolyte reaction manifests the highest instantaneous exothermic peak, though the anode-electrolyte reaction drives the overall thermal runaway trigger.
F. The sequence SEI → anode-electrolyte → cathode-electrolyte → electrolyte decomposition reflects the correct temperature-ordered progression of reactions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI decomposition occurs first at 130°C, before anode-electrolyte reaction at 150°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathode-electrolyte reaction has the highest exothermic peak, not the most cumulative heat or the trigger role; that role belongs to the anode-electrolyte reaction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly assigns the anode-electrolyte reaction as key contributor to heat generation and trigger of violent thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence assigns the highest exothermic peak to the cathode-electrolyte reaction, not electrolyte decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence distinguishes peak exotherm (cathode-electrolyte) from cumulative heat and trigger role (anode-electrolyte); both attributes are correctly assigned.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Temperature onsets 130°C, 150°C, ~184°C, ~230°C map exactly to SEI, anode-electrolyte, cathode-electrolyte, electrolyte decomposition in that order.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Temperature-ordered reaction sequence and distinction between peak exotherm versus cumulative heat/trigger role across four thermal runaway reactions
analysis.primary_boundary: LiCoO2/graphite pouch cell at high SOC under mechanical indentation; sequence and role assignments are chemistry- and condition-specific
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution (cathode vs. anode role confusion)
causal overclaim (peak exotherm equated with trigger role)
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing reaction onset temperatures to other chemistries or SOC levels
Assuming cathode-electrolyte reaction is the primary thermal runaway driver because it has the highest peak

solver_view.stem: An engineer tests two geometrically identical LiCoO2/graphite pouch cells under nail penetration. Cell A is penetrated with a fine needle; Cell B with a nail of larger cross-sectional area. Both cells are at the same high SOC. The engineer expects Cell B to reach a higher peak temperature due to greater short-circuit area. Which statements correctly evaluate this expectation and the underlying mechanism for mechanically induced thermal runaway?
options: A. The engineer's expectation is well-founded: larger nail cross-section always produces higher peak temperature because more electrode area is short-circuited.
B. Within a certain range, a larger nail cross-section may lead to incomplete energy release, producing a relatively lower temperature rise than a finer needle.
C. The internal short circuit is the dominant heat source driving thermal runaway in nail penetration scenarios.
D. The anode-electrolyte reaction, not the short-circuit current, provides the primary drastic exothermic contribution to thermal runaway in mechanically induced scenarios.
E. Transport limitation of Li+ in the solid phase reliably prevents thermal runaway in high-energy batteries by capping short-circuit current before sufficient heat accumulates.
F. The short circuit acts as an inducing factor for thermal abuse rather than the main heat generator in mechanically induced internal short-circuit events.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states larger cross-section may cause incomplete energy release and lower temperature within a certain range, contradicting a universal monotonic increase.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states larger nail cross-section can lead to incomplete energy release and relatively lower temperature rise within a certain range.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states internal short circuit has limited heat generation contribution and serves only as an inducing factor, not the dominant heat source.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly assigns the anode-electrolyte reaction as the primary drastic exothermic contributor in mechanically induced thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that for most high-energy batteries, enough heat accumulates to trigger thermal runaway before transport limitation takes effect, negating this protective claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states the short circuit serves as an inducing factor for thermal abuse with limited heat generation contribution in mechanically induced scenarios.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Counterintuitive nail-size effect on temperature and correct causal hierarchy between short-circuit current and anode-electrolyte reaction in mechanical abuse
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanically induced internal short circuit in LiCoO2/graphite pouch cells; nail-size effect is conditional ('within a certain range'); transport limitation finding applies to high-energy batteries
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim (short circuit as dominant heat source)
boundary overreach (transport limitation as reliable safety mechanism)
disallowed_extrapolations: Asserting nail-size effect holds universally beyond the stated range
Generalizing short-circuit dominance claim to non-mechanical abuse modes

solver_view.stem: A LiCoO2/graphite pouch battery is tested under mechanical indentation at varying states of charge. The study identifies a critical SOC threshold for thermal runaway triggering. Which of the following conclusions are appropriately supported given the experimental scope, and which represent inadmissible extrapolations?
options: A. For the specific battery studied, SOC 0.6 is the critical threshold below which mechanical indentation does not trigger thermal runaway.
B. SOC 0.6 is the universal critical threshold for mechanical-indentation-induced thermal runaway in all LiCoO2/graphite pouch cells.
C. The critical SOC threshold may differ for batteries with different structures or material systems, so the 0.6 value should not be applied universally.
D. At 95% SOC, thermal runaway is activated near 150°C under mechanical indentation, with temperature rapidly rising to approximately 650°C.
E. Because thermal runaway was observed at 95% SOC and not below SOC 0.6, any SOC above 0.6 guarantees thermal runaway under mechanical indentation for all lithium-ion chemistries.
F. The separator collapse and melting of active materials and current collector are consequences observed after the 650°C peak is reached at high SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SOC 0.6 is the critical point for the batteries studied, implying below this threshold thermal runaway is not triggered under this loading mode.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly qualifies that the 0.6 threshold may vary with battery structure and material system, prohibiting universal application.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states the critical SOC value may vary with battery structure and material system, supporting this qualified conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states thermal runaway activates near 150°C and temperature rises rapidly to about 650°C at 95% SOC under mechanical indentation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Extrapolates a chemistry-specific threshold to all lithium-ion chemistries; evidence explicitly limits the 0.6 threshold to the studied battery system.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states separator collapse and melting occur as thermal runaway progresses to ~650°C, not after the peak is reached; temporal framing is reversed.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility judgment on SOC threshold generalizability and correct temperature-consequence framing for mechanical indentation thermal runaway
analysis.primary_boundary: SOC threshold of 0.6 is explicitly scoped to the studied LiCoO2/graphite pouch battery; temperature and damage observations are at 95% SOC under mechanical indentation only
intended_error_modes: scale transfer overclaim (extending to all Li-ion chemistries)
causal overclaim (reversing temporal sequence of damage events)
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying SOC 0.6 threshold to other chemistries or cell formats
Claiming separator collapse occurs only after peak temperature rather than during the rise

solver_view.prompt: In a nail penetration experiment on a LiCoO2/graphite pouch cell, a coupled electro-thermal model predicts thermal runaway origination at approximately 128°C within about 3 seconds. An engineer argues that because the internal short circuit generates substantial heat at the nail site, eliminating or minimizing the short-circuit current should be the primary design strategy to prevent thermal runaway. Using the causal hierarchy established for mechanically induced thermal runaway in this chemistry, evaluate whether this design strategy is correctly prioritized and explain what the primary heat-generation mechanism is, including any known limitation on mechanisms that might otherwise self-limit the short-circuit contribution.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's prioritization is incorrect. Evidence shows that in mechanically induced internal short-circuit events, the short circuit itself has limited heat generation contribution and functions only as an inducing factor for thermal abuse. The primary drastic exothermic contributor is the anode-electrolyte reaction, which provides most of the heat driving violent thermal runaway. Therefore, design strategies should prioritize suppressing or delaying the anode-electrolyte reaction rather than focusing solely on short-circuit current reduction. Additionally, while transport limitation of Li+ in the solid phase could theoretically cap short-circuit current and heat, evidence indicates that for most high-energy batteries sufficient heat accumulates to trigger thermal runaway before this transport limitation becomes effective, further undermining reliance on short-circuit self-limitation as a safety mechanism.
must_include: short circuit has limited heat generation contribution and is only an inducing factor
anode-electrolyte reaction is the primary exothermic contributor to thermal runaway
transport limitation of Li+ in the solid phase does not reliably prevent thermal runaway in high-energy batteries because heat accumulates before it takes effect
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming short-circuit elimination fully prevents thermal runaway in all lithium-ion chemistries
Asserting transport limitation is an effective safety barrier for high-energy batteries
Generalizing the 128°C origination temperature or 3-second timeline to other cell formats or abuse modes

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Causal hierarchy between short-circuit heat and anode-electrolyte reaction heat in mechanical abuse, and admissibility of transport-limitation as a protective mechanism
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanically induced internal short circuit in LiCoO2/graphite pouch cells; short-circuit role and transport-limitation finding are scoped to this chemistry and abuse mode
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim (short circuit as dominant heat source)
boundary overreach (transport limitation as reliable safety mechanism in high-energy batteries)
regime transfer error (applying nail-penetration causal hierarchy to other abuse modes)

solver_view.stem: A cylindrical lithium-ion cell is subjected to external heating in two separate tests at identical total heating power (500 W). Test A uses a small heater covering 10% of the cell surface; Test B uses a large heater covering 40% of the cell surface. An engineer evaluates four statements about these conditions. Which statements are CORRECT?
options: A. Test A has higher heating power density than Test B because the same power is delivered over a smaller area.
B. Test B triggers thermal runaway faster than Test A because the larger heater area distributes heat more uniformly, reducing local thermal resistance.
C. Test A triggers thermal runaway faster than Test B because higher heating power density at the heater–cell interface accelerates local temperature rise.
D. Test A introduces less total flux energy into the battery before thermal runaway onset compared with Test B.
E. Both tests introduce the same total flux energy before thermal runaway because heating power is identical.
F. Test B requires more heating time to trigger thermal runaway than Test A under these conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Same power over smaller area directly yields higher power density by definition; consistent with C001.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Uniform distribution reduces local power density; C001 shows smaller area triggers TR quicker, not larger area at same power.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C001 explicitly states smaller heating area with same power triggers TR quicker due to higher local power density.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C001 states that when smaller area triggers TR quicker, less flux energy is introduced into the battery.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Equal power does not imply equal flux energy; shorter TR time in Test A means less total energy delivered before onset.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Test B has lower power density than Test A at same total power, so it takes longer to trigger TR per C001.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Effect of heating area on TR trigger time and flux energy at constant total heating power
analysis.primary_boundary: Constant total heating power condition; conclusions apply only within this constraint
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing area-effect conclusions to variable-power conditions
Claiming uniform heating is always faster

solver_view.stem: A battery engineer compares two external-heating thermal runaway tests at constant heating power density. Test X uses a heater area of 20 cm²; Test Y uses a heater area of 80 cm². The engineer also reviews data showing that at 150 W total power, flux energy to TR is 2.46×10⁵ J (heating time 1956 s), whereas at 1250 W total power, flux energy to TR is 0.45×10⁵ J (heating time 79 s). Which of the following conclusions are SUPPORTED by this evidence?
options: A. Test Y triggers thermal runaway faster than Test X because larger area at the same power density means greater total heating power.
B. Test X triggers thermal runaway faster than Test Y because smaller area concentrates heat regardless of power density.
C. Higher total heating power reduces the flux energy required to trigger thermal runaway.
D. The heating time to thermal runaway decreases as total heating power increases, based on the 150 W and 1250 W comparison.
E. At high heating power, the battery core's heat conduction capacity is exceeded, allowing the front region to reach TR temperature rapidly.
F. Flux energy required to trigger TR is independent of total heating power level.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C002 states larger area at same power density triggers TR faster because it implies larger total power.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Concentration effect applies when total power is fixed; here power density is fixed, so C001 logic does not apply.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C004 shows flux energy drops from 2.46×10⁵ J at 150 W to 0.45×10⁵ J at 1250 W, confirming inverse relationship.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C004 directly reports 1956 s at 150 W versus 79 s at 1250 W, demonstrating decreasing time with increasing power.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C005 states high heating power causes ΔPeqv to become large, allowing front battery core to reach TR temperature T2 quickly.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C004 shows flux energy varies substantially with power level, directly contradicting independence claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing constant-power-density vs. constant-total-power regimes and their effects on TR trigger time and flux energy
analysis.primary_boundary: Regime condition must be identified before applying C001 vs. C002 logic
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying constant-total-power conclusions to constant-power-density scenarios
Claiming flux energy is universal constant

solver_view.stem: An engineer designing a worst-case thermal runaway test protocol wants to minimize the time to TR onset using external heating. They consider four heater configurations on the same cell type: (P1) low power, large area; (P2) low power, small area; (P3) high power, large area; (P4) high power, small area. Based on the governing mechanism that TR onset is determined by the balance between heater flux power and battery core heat conduction, which configuration(s) are CORRECTLY ranked or characterized?
options: A. P4 produces the shortest time to TR onset because it combines maximum flux power density with maximum total power.
B. P1 produces the longest time to TR onset because low total power allows the battery core to conduct away most of the flux, prolonging heating.
C. P3 will always trigger TR faster than P4 because larger area ensures more uniform heating and avoids local heat dissipation losses.
D. P2 triggers TR faster than P1 at the same low power level because higher power density at the heater exceeds local conduction capacity sooner.
E. The battery core heat conduction power is the dominant factor only at high heating power, making conduction irrelevant at low power.
F. P4 introduces less total flux energy into the cell before TR than P1, consistent with the inverse relationship between power level and required flux energy.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C003 states high power combined with small area most effectively shortens heating time to TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C005 states at low heating power the battery core conducts most flux power, extending heating duration significantly.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C003 shows small area with high power outperforms large area; uniform heating does not guarantee faster TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C001 states at same total power, smaller area triggers TR quicker due to higher local power density exceeding conduction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C005 states conduction dominates at LOW power, not high power; this option reverses the stated regime.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C004 confirms higher power requires less flux energy before TR; P4 (high power) introduces less energy than P1 (low power).

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-variable optimization of heater configuration for worst-case TR test and underlying flux-conduction balance mechanism
analysis.primary_boundary: Conclusions apply to external heating TR trigger mechanism; conduction dominance regime is low-power, not high-power
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming uniform heating always accelerates TR
Claiming conduction is irrelevant at low power

solver_view.prompt: A test engineer observes that triggering thermal runaway at 150 W requires 1956 s and approximately 2.46×10⁵ J of flux energy, while triggering it at 1250 W requires only 79 s and 0.45×10⁵ J. Explain the physical mechanism that accounts for why higher heating power requires both less time AND less total flux energy to trigger thermal runaway. Your answer should reference the role of battery core heat conduction and the concept of equivalent flux power balance.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Thermal runaway onset is governed by the balance between the heater's flux power and the battery core's heat conduction power. At low heating power (150 W), the difference between applied flux power and the core's conduction capacity (ΔPeqv) is small, so the core conducts away most of the delivered energy, requiring a long heating duration and large total flux energy input before the TR-threshold temperature is reached. At high heating power (1250 W), ΔPeqv becomes large enough that the front region of the battery core quickly exceeds the thermal runaway temperature T2 before conduction can redistribute heat; consequently, TR is triggered in far less time and with substantially less total flux energy delivered. The reduction in required flux energy at high power reflects the fact that concentrated, rapid heating overwhelms the cell's conductive dissipation pathway.
must_include: balance between flux power and battery core heat conduction power
at low power, core conducts most flux energy, prolonging heating time
at high power, ΔPeqv is large, front core reaches TR temperature T2 quickly
higher power requires less total flux energy before TR onset
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming this mechanism applies to all TR trigger modes beyond external heating
Asserting a universal quantitative threshold for ΔPeqv without supporting data
Generalizing the 150 W and 1250 W specific values to other cell chemistries or geometries

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Flux-power vs. core-conduction balance mechanism explaining the inverse relationship between heating power and required flux energy for TR
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism is specific to external heating TR trigger mode; quantitative values are from specific test conditions only
intended_error_modes: Attributing energy reduction solely to faster chemical reactions without referencing conduction balance
Claiming higher power simply heats faster without explaining why less total energy suffices
Reversing the conduction dominance regime by stating conduction matters more at high power

solver_view.stem: A PCM-based battery thermal management system uses PCM with variable thermal conductivity. During normal 5C discharge, higher PCM thermal conductivity improves temperature uniformity. However, when thermal runaway is triggered in battery 1, PCM thermal conductivity is increased to 0.8 W/(m·K). No active water cooling is operating. Which of the following statements about this scenario are correct?
options: A. Higher PCM thermal conductivity that improves normal operation performance simultaneously increases the risk of thermal runaway propagation to adjacent batteries.
B. At PCM thermal conductivity of 0.8 W/(m·K) with no active cooling, thermal runaway in battery 1 will remain confined and not propagate to battery 2.
C. The same PCM property that reduces battery temperature during normal discharge accelerates inter-battery heat transfer during thermal runaway.
D. Thermal runaway propagation at 0.8 W/(m·K) can be prevented by increasing cooling water velocity to 0.05 m/s.
E. A water velocity of 0.01 m/s is sufficient to prevent propagation at PCM thermal conductivity of 0.8 W/(m·K).
F. The critical threshold at which adjacent battery temperature exceeds the thermal runaway onset is crossed when PCM conductivity reaches 0.8 W/(m·K).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence confirms higher PCM conductivity aids normal operation but accelerates propagation, creating a direct trade-off.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: At 0.8 W/(m·K) without cooling, battery 2 exceeds critical thermal runaway temperature, so propagation is not confined.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The same conductivity property responsible for temperature uniformity during discharge also channels runaway heat to adjacent cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states 0.05 m/s water velocity is required at 0.8 W/(m·K) to prevent propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 0.01 m/s is sufficient only at 0.4 or 0.6 W/(m·K); at 0.8 W/(m·K) a higher velocity of 0.05 m/s is required.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states battery 2 exceeds the critical thermal runaway temperature specifically when conductivity reaches 0.8 W/(m·K).

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Trade-off between PCM thermal conductivity benefits during normal operation and propagation risk during thermal runaway, combined with threshold identification.
analysis.primary_boundary: PCM thermal conductivity of 0.8 W/(m·K) is the threshold at which adjacent battery exceeds critical temperature without active cooling.
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming higher conductivity is always beneficial
Assuming 0.01 m/s water velocity is universally sufficient regardless of PCM conductivity

solver_view.stem: Three battery thermal management configurations are compared during a 5C discharge and a subsequent thermal runaway event in battery 1: System 1 uses PCM only; System 2 uses aluminum plate plus water cooling only; System 3 uses PCM combined with aluminum plate and water cooling. During 5C discharge, the allowable battery temperature difference limit is 5°C. During thermal runaway, the concern is propagation speed to battery 2. Which statements correctly characterize these systems?
options: A. System 1 meets the 5°C temperature difference requirement during normal discharge, while System 2 does not.
B. System 2 prevents thermal runaway propagation faster than System 1 because its higher thermal conductance removes heat more efficiently.
C. System 2 causes thermal runaway in both batteries to occur nearly simultaneously due to absence of effective heat insulation between them.
D. System 1 completely prevents thermal runaway propagation, only delaying it by 460 seconds.
E. System 1 delays but does not prevent thermal runaway propagation, with a 460-second interval between the two batteries.
F. System 2 satisfies the 5°C temperature difference requirement during 5C discharge.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: System 1 keeps temperature difference within 1°C; System 2 produces 14.7°C difference, exceeding the 5°C limit.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: System 2 lacks heat insulation, causing near-simultaneous propagation—it worsens propagation speed, not prevents it.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states System 2 lacks effective heat insulation, enabling rapid heat transfer and near-simultaneous thermal runaway in both batteries.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: System 1 delays propagation by 460 s but is explicitly stated to be unable to prevent it effectively.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states System 1 cannot prevent propagation but slows it, with a 460-second thermal runaway time interval.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: System 2 produces a 14.7°C temperature difference during 5C discharge, far exceeding the 5°C limit.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Comparative system behavior across two distinct operating regimes: normal discharge temperature uniformity and thermal runaway propagation dynamics.
analysis.primary_boundary: 5°C temperature difference limit during normal operation; PCM insulation role during thermal runaway propagation.
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring System 2 prevents propagation because it has active cooling
Inferring System 1 prevents propagation because it delays it

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery thermal runaway model defines the process as irreversible at 230°C and includes four sequential decomposition reactions. An engineer claims that once the battery reaches 120°C, all four reactions are simultaneously active. A second engineer claims that below 200°C, only two reactions are active. Using the defined reaction onset temperatures, which claims are correct?
options: A. The first engineer's claim is incorrect; at 120°C only two reactions are active: SEI decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction.
B. The second engineer's claim is correct; positive-electrolyte and electrolyte decomposition reactions require at least 200°C and 230°C respectively.
C. At exactly 120°C, three reactions are active: SEI decomposition, negative-electrolyte, and positive-electrolyte reactions.
D. The thermal runaway process becomes irreversible at 200°C when the positive-electrolyte reaction initiates.
E. SEI decomposition is the first reaction to activate and begins at or above 70°C, preceding all other decomposition reactions.
F. Electrolyte decomposition is active at 200°C because it is the final stage of a continuous decomposition sequence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 120°C, SEI (onset ≥70°C) and negative-electrolyte (onset ≥120°C) are active; positive-electrolyte requires ≥200°C and electrolyte decomposition ≥230°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Positive-electrolyte onset is ≥200°C and electrolyte decomposition onset is ≥230°C, confirming both are inactive below 200°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Positive-electrolyte reaction requires ≥200°C; at 120°C it is not yet active, so only two reactions are running.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The model defines irreversibility at 230°C, not 200°C. Positive-electrolyte reaction onset at 200°C does not equal the irreversibility threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI decomposition onset is ≥70°C, the lowest threshold among the four reactions, making it the first to activate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Electrolyte decomposition onset is ≥230°C; at 200°C it has not yet activated regardless of sequential reasoning.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Correct identification of which decomposition reactions are active at specific temperatures using defined onset thresholds.
analysis.primary_boundary: Each reaction has a distinct onset temperature: SEI ≥70°C, negative-electrolyte ≥120°C, positive-electrolyte ≥200°C, electrolyte decomposition ≥230°C; irreversibility defined at 230°C.
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating reaction sequence as simultaneous activation
Equating positive-electrolyte onset with irreversibility threshold

solver_view.prompt: A battery module uses a hybrid thermal management system combining PCM with aluminum plate and water cooling. An engineer proposes increasing PCM thermal conductivity from 0.4 W/(m·K) to 0.8 W/(m·K) to improve temperature uniformity during normal operation, while keeping the cooling water velocity at 0.01 m/s. Explain whether this design change is admissible from a thermal runaway propagation perspective, identifying the specific mechanism by which the change affects propagation risk and the minimum corrective action required if the change is made.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The proposed change is not admissible without a corrective action. At PCM thermal conductivity of 0.4 W/(m·K), a water velocity of 0.01 m/s is sufficient to prevent thermal runaway propagation. However, increasing PCM conductivity to 0.8 W/(m·K) enhances inter-battery heat transfer during a thermal runaway event, causing the adjacent battery temperature to exceed the critical thermal runaway threshold even with active cooling at 0.01 m/s. The mechanism is that higher PCM conductivity, while beneficial for temperature uniformity during normal discharge, accelerates heat conduction from a runaway cell to neighboring cells during a fault event. To maintain propagation prevention at 0.8 W/(m·K), the cooling water velocity must be increased to at least 0.05 m/s.
must_include: 0.01 m/s is insufficient at 0.8 W/(m·K) PCM conductivity
higher PCM conductivity accelerates heat transfer to adjacent battery during thermal runaway
cooling water velocity must be increased to 0.05 m/s to prevent propagation at 0.8 W/(m·K)
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming 0.01 m/s is universally sufficient regardless of PCM conductivity
Claiming the design change is admissible without increasing water velocity
Asserting that higher PCM conductivity always prevents propagation by improving heat removal

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility judgment combining PCM conductivity threshold effect on propagation with the required corrective water velocity, requiring two-variable reasoning.
analysis.primary_boundary: At PCM conductivity 0.8 W/(m·K), water velocity of 0.01 m/s is insufficient; 0.05 m/s is the minimum required to prevent propagation.

solver_view.stem: A cylindrical lithium-ion cell is subjected to lateral compression at two loading rates: quasi-static (0.5 mm/min) and dynamic (60 mm/min). An engineer compares the mechanical response across both conditions and reviews sensor-based failure detection. Which of the following statements are supported by experimental evidence from this loading configuration?
options: A. Under quasi-static compression, the electrolyte contributes negligibly to the mechanical resistance of the cell.
B. Under dynamic compression, the electrolyte substantially increases the apparent battery strength relative to the quasi-static case.
C. The shell casing is the dominant load-bearing component, accounting for the majority of peak lateral compression force.
D. At a load rate of 60 mm/min, the equivalent strength values of cells from different manufacturers converge toward a consistent level.
E. Voltage and temperature sensors reliably detect the onset of internal structural damage before irreversible thermal runaway begins.
F. The elastic-phase equivalent strength scales proportionally with load rate, so a 120-fold rate increase produces roughly a 120-fold strength increase.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states electrolyte has little influence on compression under quasi-static conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states electrolyte greatly increases battery strength specifically under dynamic compression.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Shell casing contributes only 0.3–0.6% of peak lateral force; it is not the dominant load-bearing component.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that at 60 mm/min load rate, equivalent strength of Sony and Samsung batteries tends to be consistent.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states electrical/thermal sensor signals may indicate battery has already entered irreversible thermal runaway, not early detection.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A 120-fold rate increase yields only 6–25% strength increase in the elastic phase, not proportional scaling.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Condition-dependent electrolyte mechanical role and rate-sensitivity limits of equivalent strength
analysis.primary_boundary: Quasi-static vs. dynamic compression regime distinction; elastic-phase rate sensitivity is disproportionately small
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing dynamic electrolyte stiffening to quasi-static regime
Assuming proportional rate-strength scaling beyond reported data

solver_view.stem: During mechanical abuse testing of a multi-layer pouch cell using a hemispherical punch, three failure events are recorded sequentially: mechanical soft short-circuit, electrical soft short-circuit, and thermal soft short-circuit. An engineer evaluates whether voltage/temperature monitoring alone is sufficient for early failure warning, particularly for large-format cells. Which statements are INCORRECT regarding failure sequencing and detection adequacy?
options: A. Mechanical soft short-circuit precedes electrical soft short-circuit by less than 1 mm of displacement in both hemispherical punch and rigid rod tests.
B. Because voltage drops before temperature rises, voltage monitoring alone provides sufficient early warning of internal structural damage.
C. For large-format cells, SOC changes immediately when internal structure is first damaged, enabling reliable early detection via electrical signals.
D. The cascading failure of multi-layer separator structure means individual separator failures can be detected and arrested sequentially.
E. When electrical and thermal sensor signals are first detected, the battery may already be in an irreversible thermal runaway stage.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Mechanical soft short-circuit leads electrical by 0.88 mm and 0.75 mm respectively, both under 1 mm; statement is accurate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (as required)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states voltage and temperature signals alone are insufficient; sensor detection may lag behind irreversible runaway onset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (as required)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states SOC does not change immediately upon internal structural damage, especially for large-format cells.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (as required)
option_judgments.justification: Cascading effect causes multi-layer structure to fail almost simultaneously, making sequential detection and arrest unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states that sensor-detected electrical/thermal signals may indicate irreversible thermal runaway is already underway.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Failure sequence ordering and the detection gap between mechanical damage onset and sensor-observable signals
analysis.primary_boundary: Sensor signals lag mechanical damage; cascading separator failure is near-simultaneous, not sequential
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming electrical signal precedes mechanical damage
Generalizing sequential detection feasibility from single-layer to multi-layer systems

solver_view.stem: A battery engineer is designing a crash safety system for an EV battery pack. Historical data shows over 14% of EV accidents involve collision and compression. The engineer considers the mechanical behavior of cylindrical cells under lateral compression at varying load rates and evaluates which design inferences are justified. Select all statements that are supported within their stated conditions.
options: A. Collision-induced compression is a statistically significant accident trigger for EVs, justifying mechanical abuse as a relevant test scenario.
B. Because shell casing force is only 0.3–0.6% of peak battery force under lateral compression, casing redesign alone cannot substantially increase peak crush resistance.
C. Increasing load rate from quasi-static to dynamic conditions uniformly doubles the equivalent strength across all deformation stages.
D. Under dynamic compression, the deformation behavior in the final stage differs from quasi-static behavior due to shell casing damage and energy absorption.
E. The convergence of equivalent strength at high load rates implies that battery chemistry differences become irrelevant at all dynamic loading conditions.
F. Mechanical failure precedes detectable electrical failure during punch indentation, suggesting mechanical sensing could provide earlier warning than voltage monitoring.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Over 14% of EV accidents involve collision/compression, directly supporting mechanical abuse as a relevant test scenario.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Shell casing contributes only 0.3–0.6% of peak lateral force; redesigning it cannot substantially alter peak crush resistance.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 120-fold rate increase yields only 6–25% elastic-phase strength increase; uniform doubling is unsupported and contradicts reported data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states Stage IV deformation under dynamic compression differs significantly from quasi-static due to casing damage and energy absorption.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Strength convergence is reported at 60 mm/min; extending this to all dynamic conditions is an unsupported universal extrapolation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Mechanical soft short-circuit precedes electrical soft short-circuit by 0.75–0.88 mm, supporting earlier warning via mechanical sensing.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Design inference validity from component-level mechanical data to system-level safety strategy
analysis.primary_boundary: Shell casing contribution is negligible; rate-strength relationship is disproportionate; strength convergence is threshold-specific not universal
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming chemistry independence at all dynamic rates from a single threshold observation
Claiming uniform strength doubling from disproportionate rate data

solver_view.prompt: During mechanical abuse testing of lithium-ion cells, three types of soft short-circuit events are observed: mechanical, electrical, and thermal. Experimental data show that mechanical soft short-circuit occurs approximately 0.75–0.88 mm of displacement ahead of electrical soft short-circuit, and significantly ahead of thermal soft short-circuit. Separately, evidence shows that when electrical and thermal sensor signals are first detected, the battery may already be in an irreversible thermal runaway stage, and that SOC does not change immediately when internal structure is damaged—particularly in large-format cells. Given these findings, explain why relying solely on voltage and temperature monitoring is insufficient for early failure detection, and identify what type of sensing approach is better supported by the evidence.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Voltage and temperature monitoring are insufficient because the sensor-detectable signals (electrical and thermal) lag behind the actual onset of internal mechanical damage. Mechanical soft short-circuit precedes electrical soft short-circuit by 0.75–0.88 mm of displacement, meaning structural failure is already underway before any voltage change is measurable. Furthermore, SOC does not respond immediately to internal structural damage in large-format cells, creating a detection gap. By the time electrical or thermal signals are registered, the battery may already be in an irreversible thermal runaway stage. The evidence supports that mechanical sensing—capable of detecting the mechanical soft short-circuit event earlier in the displacement sequence—would provide earlier warning than voltage or temperature monitoring alone.
must_include: mechanical soft short-circuit precedes electrical soft short-circuit by 0.75–0.88 mm
SOC does not change immediately upon internal structural damage
electrical/thermal sensor detection may coincide with irreversible thermal runaway onset
mechanical sensing provides earlier warning than voltage or temperature monitoring
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming mechanical sensing can prevent thermal runaway in all cases
Asserting that voltage monitoring provides zero useful information under any condition
Generalizing the 0.75–0.88 mm lead distance to all cell formats or loading geometries beyond the tested configurations

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain linking mechanical damage sequence to sensor detection lag and its implication for failure warning strategy
analysis.primary_boundary: Detection gap exists between mechanical damage onset and electrical/thermal signal emergence; SOC insensitivity is specific to large-format cells and immediate post-damage window
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim that voltage monitoring is entirely useless
scale_transfer_overclaim generalizing displacement lead to all geometries
boundary_overreach claiming mechanical sensing guarantees runaway prevention

solver_view.stem: An internal short-circuit is induced in two lithium-ion cells—one small-format and one large-format—under identical contact area conditions where the electrical contact area is exceedingly large. Both cells are at the same SOC. Which of the following statements correctly characterize the outcome differences between the two cell formats?
options: A. The small-format cell releases its energy within milliseconds, and current density does not rise to values large enough to generate significant heat.
B. The large-format cell retains sufficient energy over the same timescale to generate heat and trigger follow-on reactions.
C. The small-format cell reaches a higher maximum local temperature than the large-format cell because faster energy release concentrates heat locally.
D. Both cells exhibit identical thermal runaway risk because contact area, not cell format, is the sole determinant of heat generation.
E. The large-format cell releases energy faster than the small-format cell due to its lower internal resistance per unit area.
F. A very small contact area in either format is more likely to cause thermal runaway than a very large contact area because heat dissipation is slower relative to generation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states small-format cell releases energy within milliseconds at exceedingly large contact area before current density rises sufficiently to generate heat.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states large-format cell retains sufficient energy across the same timescale to generate heat and cause follow-on reactions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states larger contact area produces lower maximum local temperature due to faster energy release; small-format cell does not reach higher temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
