Two observations are reported: (1) the TR propagation time interval increases up to the sixth cell then decreases and stabilizes; (2) in the final parallel submodule, the current through the TR cell is approximately zero. Using these observations, explain the two-phase propagation time pattern and identify the mechanism responsible for near-zero current in the final submodule. State any boundary on generalizing the current observation.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The two-phase pattern arises because cells near the heater are pre-heated before their TR onset, slowing apparent propagation (increasing time interval through cell 6). As TR advances, cumulative heat from multiple preceding TR cells accelerates subsequent cell TR onset, reducing and stabilizing the propagation interval in later cells. Near-zero current in the final submodule occurs because the accumulated thermal pre-loading from all preceding TR events causes the final TR cell to enter TR so rapidly that there is insufficient time for electricity to redistribute from adjacent healthy cells. This mechanism is specific to the final submodule position under the tested open-chamber conditions; earlier submodules showed measurable current transfer, so the zero-current behavior cannot be generalized to all submodule positions.
must_include: pre-heating of early cells explains increasing propagation time interval up to cell 6
cumulative thermal acceleration from preceding TR cells explains decrease and stabilization after cell 6
near-zero current in final submodule caused by rapid TR onset leaving insufficient time for electricity transfer
boundary: zero-current observation is specific to the final submodule position, not all submodules
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming zero-current behavior applies to all submodules or all connection types universally
Attributing the propagation time pattern solely to electrical effects rather than thermal pre-heating
Generalizing the combustion-irrelevance finding to enclosed battery pack environments

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal reasoning linking thermal pre-heating to non-monotonic propagation time pattern, and cumulative thermal loading to suppressed current transfer in the final submodule
analysis.primary_boundary: Observations are bounded to open-chamber 12-cell module experiments; final-submodule zero-current mechanism is position-specific and not universal across submodules
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim generalizing zero-current to all submodules
mechanism_substitution attributing time pattern to electrical rather than thermal effects
regime_transfer_error applying open-chamber findings to enclosed systems

solver_view.stem: A cylindrical lithium-ion battery is subjected to quasi-static axial compression. Displacement is monitored from 0 to full failure. During the first half of compression (displacement < 4 mm), force increases linearly. Beyond 4 mm, the force-displacement profile changes character. A multi-signal warning system records mechanical, electrical, and thermal soft short-circuit events. Which of the following statements correctly describe the signal ordering and its causal basis? Select ALL that apply.
options: A. The mechanical soft short-circuit signal occurs first because the first derivative of force with respect to displacement immediately reflects internal structural damage.
B. The thermal soft short-circuit signal occurs first because ohmic heating is generated at the short-circuit site before mechanical deformation is measurable.
C. The electrical soft short-circuit signal occurs after the mechanical signal but before the thermal signal under typical large-capacity battery conditions.
D. The linear force growth below 4 mm displacement is caused by plastic deformation of the jellyroll active material layers.
E. In small batteries, electrical and thermal warning weights can be similar because ohmic heat transfers quickly to the surface, allowing simultaneous voltage drop and temperature rise.
F. The thermal signal always lags all other signals regardless of battery size or capacity.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Mechanical characteristics immediately reflect the decline in force-displacement first derivative when internal structure is slightly damaged.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal signal lags mechanical signal; ohmic heating does not precede mechanical deformation detection.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Electrical soft short-circuit lags mechanical but precedes thermal in large-capacity batteries where voltage is maintained initially.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Linear force growth below 4 mm is caused by extrusion of space between shell casing and jellyroll, not jellyroll plastic deformation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: In small batteries, rapid surface heat transfer makes voltage drop and temperature rise observable simultaneously, equalizing electrical and thermal weights.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal lag is size-dependent; in small batteries electrical and thermal weights can be similar, so universal lag is an overclaim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Signal ordering causal chain and size-dependent boundary conditions for thermal warning weight
analysis.primary_boundary: Large-capacity vs. small-battery distinction governs whether thermal and electrical weights are similar or distinct
disallowed_extrapolations: Thermal always lags regardless of battery size
Linear force region attributed to jellyroll deformation

solver_view.stem: A battery module contains multiple cylindrical cells. In Test 1, all batteries are at SOC=1.0. In Test 2, batteries not in direct contact with the external load are reduced to SOC=0.5. In Test 3, those rear batteries are at SOC=0.2. The battery directly contacting the load remains at SOC=1.0 in all tests. Average failure displacement is recorded. Which statements about this experimental configuration are CORRECT? Select ALL that apply.
options: A. Failure displacement increases by approximately 14.2% in Test 2 and 16.5% in Test 3 relative to Test 1.
B. The SOC of the battery directly contacting the external load is reduced in Tests 2 and 3 to achieve the displacement improvement.
C. The improvement in failure displacement from Test 2 to Test 3 is achievable in practice because rear batteries can be selectively discharged without affecting front batteries.
D. At high module SOC, strain is continuously transmitted and batteries almost fail simultaneously, with top-layer batteries showing the largest failure degree.
E. Under low SOC rear-battery conditions, only high-SOC batteries and those in limited space behind the load fail, with other batteries remaining relatively stable.
F. Reducing rear battery SOC eliminates thermal runaway entirely in all module configurations.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Reported failure displacement increases are 14.2% for SOC=0.5 rear batteries and 16.5% for SOC=0.2 rear batteries vs. all-SOC=1 baseline.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: It is generally impossible to change SOC of batteries directly contacting the load since they fail first; only rear batteries are modified.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The limitation applies only to front-contact batteries; rear batteries can be selectively discharged, making the strategy practically feasible.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At high SOC, strain is continuously transmitted and all batteries nearly fail simultaneously; top-layer contact batteries show largest failure degree.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: After rapid SOC reduction of rear batteries, strain discontinuity limits failure to high-SOC and space-constrained batteries; others remain stable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Low SOC makes violent thermal runaway difficult but does not eliminate it; maximum temperature is capped near 150°C, not zero.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent failure displacement improvement and module-level strain propagation behavior
analysis.primary_boundary: SOC reduction applies only to rear batteries not in direct contact with load; front battery SOC cannot practically be changed
disallowed_extrapolations: SOC reduction eliminates thermal runaway entirely
Front-contact battery SOC can be modified

solver_view.stem: A cylindrical battery is tested under rigid rod punch and hemispherical punch configurations at two SOC levels: SOC=0.2 and SOC=0.6. Battery strength, punch force, and post-test thermal behavior are recorded. A simulation model neglects the metal shell contribution. Which of the following statements are CORRECT regarding SOC effects and modeling assumptions? Select ALL that apply.
options: A. At SOC=0.2, neither punch geometry produces violent thermal runaway, and maximum temperatures remain below 120°C for both tests.
B. At SOC=0.6, both punch configurations produce severe gas injection and thermal runaway with maximum temperatures exceeding 300°C.
C. Battery strength and peak force decrease as SOC increases because lithium extraction weakens the negative electrode lattice.
D. Neglecting the metal shell in simulation is justified because its resistance contributes only 0.3%–0.6% of the crest force.
E. The hemispherical punch produces a higher maximum temperature than the rigid rod punch at SOC=0.2, but both remain below the thermal runaway threshold.
F. The SOC-dependent force increase is caused by lithium ion embedding in the positive electrode.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At SOC=0.2, rigid rod gives 69.3°C and hemispherical gives 119.1°C; no violent thermal runaway and structure remains intact.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At SOC=0.6, both tests produce severe gas injection, thermal runaway, temperatures exceeding 300°C, and severe structural damage.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Battery strength and force INCREASE with SOC due to more lithium ions embedded in the negative electrode, not decrease.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Metal shell resistance is only 0.3%–0.6% of crest force, justifying its omission from simulation without significant accuracy loss.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Hemispherical punch yields 119.1°C vs. rigid rod 69.3°C at SOC=0.2; both below violent thermal runaway threshold with intact structure.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SOC-dependent force increase is caused by lithium embedding in the NEGATIVE electrode, not the positive electrode.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent mechanical strength direction and punch-geometry thermal outcome differentiation
analysis.primary_boundary: SOC=0.2 stays below thermal runaway; SOC=0.6 crosses it for both punch types
disallowed_extrapolations: Strength decreases with SOC
Force increase attributed to positive electrode

solver_view.prompt: A battery module engineer proposes two safety strategies: (1) reduce the SOC of ALL batteries in a module before a potential mechanical abuse event, and (2) reduce only the SOC of batteries NOT in direct contact with the external mechanical load. Using the evidence on module-level failure behavior, signal warning weights, and SOC effects, explain which strategy is more practically feasible, what mechanical and thermal outcomes each strategy produces at the module level, and identify one fundamental limitation that constrains Strategy 1 but not Strategy 2.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Strategy 2 is more practically feasible. Batteries directly contacting the external load typically fail first during mechanical abuse, making it generally impossible to reduce their SOC in time—this is the fundamental limitation of Strategy 1 that does not apply to Strategy 2. For Strategy 2, reducing rear battery SOC to medium increases module failure displacement by roughly 12–14% and to low SOC by a further 13–17% across 6-, 9-, and 20-battery modules. Under low SOC rear-battery conditions, strain transmission becomes discontinuous, limiting failure to high-SOC front batteries and space-constrained cells; remaining batteries stay relatively stable with maximum temperatures not exceeding 150°C, making violent thermal runaway difficult. Strategy 1, if achievable, would also reduce failure severity, but the front-contact battery constraint makes full-module SOC reduction impractical under real abuse scenarios.
must_include: Front-contact batteries fail first, making their SOC reduction impractical
Rear battery SOC reduction increases failure displacement (quantitative range supported)
Low SOC rear-battery state limits strain continuity and caps maximum temperature near 150°C
Violent thermal runaway becomes difficult but is not stated to be fully eliminated
disallowed_extrapolations: Strategy 1 is impossible under all conditions—only stated as generally impractical for front-contact batteries
Low SOC completely eliminates thermal runaway
Failure displacement improvements apply equally to front-contact batteries

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Practical feasibility boundary of SOC-based mechanical abuse mitigation and module-level thermal-mechanical outcome prediction
analysis.primary_boundary: SOC reduction is feasible for rear batteries but generally not for front-contact batteries due to failure sequencing
intended_error_modes: boundary_overreach claiming full SOC reduction is always possible
causal_overclaim that low SOC eliminates thermal runaway entirely
scale_transfer_overclaim applying single-cell SOC effects directly to module without accounting for strain transmission change

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell uses a polyethylene (PE) separator and is subjected to a thermal abuse event. The cell temperature rises from 80°C through 200°C. An engineer claims the separator provides a shutdown effect and then maintains structural integrity up to 200°C. Which statements correctly characterize separator behavior across this temperature range?
options: A. PE separator pore closure produces a shutdown effect near 130°C, increasing cell resistance and making the melting process endothermic.
B. PE separator structural collapse is prevented up to 200°C because the shutdown effect permanently arrests thermal runaway.
C. A ceramic-coated separator can maintain structural integrity at 200°C, whereas an uncoated PE separator cannot.
D. The shutdown effect from PE melting guarantees that thermal runaway will not propagate beyond 130°C in any cell configuration.
E. PP separator melts at approximately 170°C, which is higher than the PE melting point of approximately 130°C.
F. Separator melting is an exothermic process that accelerates heat generation during thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: PE melts near 130°C, closing pores and raising resistance; melting is explicitly described as endothermic.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Uncoated PE collapses near 130°C; claiming structural integrity to 200°C is a boundary overreach unsupported by evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Ceramic-coated separator collapse temperature reaches 200–260°C, well above uncoated PE at ~130°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Shutdown effect is a resistance increase, not a guarantee against runaway propagation; causal overclaim beyond evidence scope.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: PP melting point ~170°C versus PE ~130°C is directly stated, confirming PP has higher thermal tolerance.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Separator melting is explicitly endothermic, not exothermic; reverses the reported thermal character of the process.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separator thermal behavior: shutdown mechanism, melting temperature thresholds, endothermic character, and ceramic coating benefit
analysis.primary_boundary: PE collapse at ~130°C vs. ceramic-coated separator integrity up to 200–260°C; melting is endothermic not exothermic
disallowed_extrapolations: Shutdown effect guarantees runaway arrest
PE maintains integrity beyond its melting point without ceramic coating

solver_view.stem: An engineer compares two NCM cathode chemistries—NCM111 and a high-Ni NCM with 85% Ni content—for use in a high-energy-density application. Decomposition enthalpy for NCM111 is 512.5 J·g⁻¹ and for Ni₀.₈₅ composition is 971.5 J·g⁻¹. The engineer also notes that LFP has an onset decomposition temperature of 310°C while NCA onset is ~160–170°C. Which of the following conclusions are supported by this evidence?
options: A. Higher Ni content in NCM cathodes correlates with greater decomposition enthalpy, indicating reduced thermal stability.
B. NCM111 is safer than high-Ni NCM solely because its decomposition enthalpy is lower, regardless of other cell components.
C. LFP cathode offers a higher onset decomposition temperature than NCA, consistent with LFP ranking as the most thermally stable common cathode.
D. The nearly twofold increase in decomposition enthalpy from NCM111 to Ni₀.₈₅ implies that cell-level thermal runaway severity doubles proportionally.
E. Materials designed for higher energy density may exhibit lower thermal stability, creating a trade-off relevant to cathode selection.
F. NCA onset temperature of ~160–170°C is lower than LCO onset of ~200°C, placing NCA as less thermally stable than LCO among those two.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states larger Ni proportion releases more heat; higher enthalpy indicates lower thermal stability.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathode enthalpy alone does not determine overall cell safety; other components contribute, making this a scale transfer overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP onset at 310°C vs. NCA at ~160–170°C; LFP is explicitly ranked most thermally stable among common cathodes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cell-level runaway severity depends on multiple components and chain reactions; proportional doubling is an unsupported system-level extrapolation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states higher energy density materials may have lower thermal stability, directly supporting this trade-off framing.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NCA onset ~160–170°C < LCO onset ~200°C; thermal stability order LCO > NCA is consistent with the stated ranking.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Ni-content effect on NCM decomposition enthalpy; cathode thermal stability ranking; energy density vs. stability trade-off
analysis.primary_boundary: Cathode-level enthalpy data cannot be directly mapped to cell-level runaway severity without accounting for full chain reaction
disallowed_extrapolations: Cell runaway severity scales proportionally with cathode enthalpy alone
Cathode enthalpy is the sole determinant of cell safety

solver_view.stem: During overdischarge of a lithium-ion cell, the anode potential reaches 3.5 V. A separate nail penetration test on a similar cell shows that ~75% of total current flows through the electrode route (not through the nail) and ~70% of cell energy releases within 60 seconds. Which statements about these two abuse scenarios are correctly supported?
options: A. In overdischarge, copper dissolution from the anode current collector begins when the anode potential reaches 3.5 V, and migrated Cu ions can form dendrites causing internal short circuit.
B. In nail penetration, the nail itself carries the majority of the short-circuit current because it provides the lowest-resistance path.
C. Nail penetration at the electrode edge is more thermally hazardous than penetration at the center due to insufficient heat dissipation at the edge.
D. Overdischarge-induced copper dendrite growth causes immediate internal short circuit within seconds of the anode reaching 3.5 V.
E. The rapid energy release during nail penetration (~70% within 60 s) implies that edge penetration poses greater thermal risk than center penetration.
F. Internal short circuit is a common outcome for both overdischarge and mechanical abuse conditions such as nail penetration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states Cu dissolves at 3.5 V anode potential, ions migrate, and dendrites can cause ISC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states ~75% of current flows through electrode routes, not the nail; reverses the reported current distribution.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states edge penetration is more dangerous because heat dissipation is insufficient at the electrode edge.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Spontaneous ISC from dendrite growth can take days to months; immediate ISC within seconds is unsupported and contradicts latency evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Combining rapid energy release (~70% in 60 s) with insufficient edge heat dissipation supports greater thermal risk at the edge.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states ISC is the most common feature for all abuse conditions, including mechanical and electrical abuse.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Current distribution in nail penetration; overdischarge Cu dissolution mechanism; ISC as universal abuse outcome; edge vs. center hazard
analysis.primary_boundary: Nail carries minority (~25%) of current; Cu dendrite ISC has latency of days to months, not seconds
disallowed_extrapolations: Nail is primary current path during penetration
Cu dendrite ISC is instantaneous upon reaching dissolution potential

solver_view.prompt: A battery engineer is evaluating two failure modes that can lead to internal short circuit (ISC) in lithium-ion cells: (1) spontaneous ISC originating from manufacturing defects or contamination, and (2) overdischarge-induced ISC via copper dendrite formation. For each pathway, describe the initiating condition, the physical mechanism leading to ISC, and one key difference in the time scale over which ISC with obvious heat generation develops. Then explain why ISC is considered a unifying feature across mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuse conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Spontaneous ISC initiates from contamination or defects introduced during manufacturing. These defects slowly evolve within the cell, and it can take several days to months before ISC with obvious heat generation develops. Overdischarge-induced ISC initiates when the anode potential reaches 3.5 V, causing copper to dissolve from the anode current collector into the electrolyte as Cu²⁺ ions. These ions migrate through the separator and deposit as copper dendrites on the cathode side; unsuppressed dendrite growth can eventually penetrate the separator and cause ISC. The key time-scale difference is that spontaneous ISC has a latency of days to months, while overdischarge-induced dendrite ISC is driven by electrochemical conditions that develop during the discharge event itself. ISC is considered a unifying feature because, regardless of whether the triggering abuse is mechanical, electrical, or thermal, the resulting damage to cell internal structure converges on a short-circuit condition between electrodes, making ISC the common proximate cause of thermal runaway across all abuse modes.
must_include: Manufacturing defects or contamination as the origin of spontaneous ISC
Days to months latency for spontaneous ISC with obvious heat generation
Anode potential reaching 3.5 V as the trigger for copper dissolution
Cu²⁺ ion migration through separator and dendrite formation on cathode side
ISC as the common feature for mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuse conditions
disallowed_extrapolations: Overdischarge-induced ISC occurs within seconds of reaching 3.5 V anode potential
Spontaneous ISC always leads to thermal runaway within a predictable time window
ISC guarantees thermal runaway in all cell chemistries and configurations
The SOC-to-heat-generation correlation for ISC is linear

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanistic distinction between spontaneous and overdischarge-induced ISC pathways; time-scale differences; ISC as universal abuse convergence point
analysis.primary_boundary: Spontaneous ISC latency is days to months; overdischarge ISC requires anode potential to reach 3.5 V dissolution threshold; ISC universality is across abuse modes, not a guarantee of runaway
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim linking ISC directly to guaranteed runaway
regime_transfer_error conflating spontaneous and overdischarge pathways

solver_view.stem: A Samsung 18650 lithium-ion battery at 100% SOC is subjected to thermal runaway under 200 W heating and 1.5 L/s intake speed. Tests are run in air versus argon atmosphere. Which of the following statements about gas concentrations are supported by the experimental evidence? Select all that apply.
options: A. Peak CO concentration in argon is approximately 50% lower than in air.
B. Peak CO2 concentration in argon (~961 ppm) is approximately 50% lower than in air (~1989 ppm).
C. Removing oxygen eliminates CO2 production entirely during thermal runaway.
D. Both CO and CO2 reductions in argon are attributable to the absence of oxygen support for combustion.
E. CO2 concentration in air exceeds CO2 concentration in argon by more than a factor of three.
F. The atmosphere type affects CO more than CO2 in absolute percentage reduction terms.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states peak CO changed by nearly 50% between argon and air atmospheres under these exact conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly reports 1989 ppm (air) vs 961 ppm (argon), a drop close to 50% for CO2.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Argon reduces CO2 by ~50% but does not eliminate it; 961 ppm CO2 is still measured in argon.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes CO reduction to absence of oxygen support, and CO2 follows the same atmospheric condition shift.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 1989/961 ≈ 2.07, not greater than three; this reverses the magnitude of the reported ratio.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence reports approximately equal ~50% reductions for both CO and CO2; no differential magnitude claim is supported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Atmosphere-dependent gas generation during thermal runaway: magnitude and mechanism of CO and CO2 reduction in argon vs air.
analysis.primary_boundary: Conditions fixed at 100% SOC, 200 W, 1.5 L/s; results are not generalizable beyond this configuration without additional evidence.
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming oxygen removal eliminates all CO2
Claiming CO is more affected than CO2 in percentage terms
Generalizing atmosphere effect to other SOC or power levels

solver_view.stem: In a thermal runaway study on lithium-ion batteries using external electric heating, the following variables are independently varied: (i) SOC level, (ii) heating power, (iii) ambient temperature, and (iv) argon gas flow rate. Which statements correctly describe how these variables affect CO and CO2 concentrations or measurement validity? Select all that apply.
options: A. Decreasing SOC monotonically decreases both CO and CO2 concentrations during thermal runaway.
B. Decreasing heating power increases CO concentration due to slower, more incomplete combustion.
C. At 180 °C ambient temperature, CO concentration is higher than at 220 °C because conditions near valve-opening temperature promote full electrolyte-membrane reaction.
D. Increasing argon flow rate from 1.5 to 2.0 L/s improves measurement accuracy by diluting gas species more uniformly.
E. At 2.0 L/s argon flow, battery temperature drops significantly, causing measurement deviation.
F. CO and CO2 concentrations both decrease as heating power decreases.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states CO and CO2 concentrations decrease as SOC decreases across different SOC conditions tested.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows CO decreases with decreasing heating power; attributing a CO increase to incomplete combustion contradicts reported trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes higher CO at 180 °C to proximity to valve-opening temperature enabling full electrolyte-membrane fusion reaction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states 2.0 L/s causes measurement deviation via battery cooling, not improved accuracy; this reverses the reported outcome.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states that at 2.0 L/s argon, battery temperature decreased significantly due to large airflow, causing measurement deviation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states both CO and CO2 concentrations decrease with decreasing electric heating power.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-variable effects on thermal runaway gas generation and measurement validity, including a non-monotonic temperature effect and a flow-rate measurement artifact.
analysis.primary_boundary: Each variable effect is reported independently; interaction effects across variables are not evidenced.
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming 2.0 L/s improves accuracy
Claiming lower heating power increases CO via incomplete combustion
Generalizing 180 °C anomaly to all temperatures below 220 °C

solver_view.stem: A safety monitoring system tracks CO concentration uncertainty during lithium-ion battery thermal runaway. Engineers observe that synthesis uncertainty of CO concentration is much greater in the initial stage of thermal runaway than after the outlet valve opens. Which conclusions are admissible based on this observation? Select all that apply.
options: A. Rising CO concentration uncertainty in the initial stage can serve as a predictive indicator of impending thermal runaway.
B. The uncertainty pattern confirms that CO is the dominant hazardous gas species throughout the entire thermal runaway event.
C. Once the outlet valve opens, CO concentration uncertainty decreases, indicating a more stable gas emission phase.
D. CO uncertainty monitoring can replace direct temperature measurement as the primary thermal runaway detection method.
E. The differential uncertainty between pre-valve and post-valve stages enables identification of a threshold beyond which thermal runaway risk is elevated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that when uncertainty reaches a certain level, possible thermal runaway can be predicted from CO concentration uncertainty changes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence addresses CO uncertainty patterns but makes no claim about CO being the dominant hazardous species across the full event.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly contrasts higher uncertainty in the initial stage with lower uncertainty during the open-valve stage, implying relative stabilization.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence supports CO uncertainty as a predictive indicator but does not claim it replaces temperature measurement; this is an unsupported system-level overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that safety uncertainty from CO can be determined and that reaching a certain uncertainty level predicts thermal runaway, implying a threshold-based risk criterion.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of CO uncertainty as a thermal runaway predictive signal versus overclaims about its role relative to other detection methods or gas species.
analysis.primary_boundary: Evidence supports CO uncertainty as a predictive indicator; it does not support claims of dominance over other gases or replacement of temperature sensing.
disallowed_extrapolations: CO uncertainty replaces temperature monitoring
CO is confirmed dominant hazardous species throughout full event

solver_view.prompt: In a thermal runaway experiment on a Samsung 18650 battery at 100% SOC, an engineer observes that CO concentration is higher at an ambient temperature of 180 °C than at 220 °C. Separately, when argon flow rate is increased from 1.5 L/s to 2.0 L/s, battery temperature drops substantially and measurement results deviate. Explain the distinct physical mechanisms responsible for each anomaly and identify what boundary condition each finding imposes on experimental design and data interpretation.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The 180 °C CO anomaly occurs because this temperature is close to the battery valve-opening temperature, at which the electrolyte and internal membrane undergo full fusion reaction, generating elevated CO. This is a non-monotonic temperature effect driven by a specific internal reaction threshold, not a general trend. Designers must not assume CO increases monotonically with ambient temperature. The 2.0 L/s flow anomaly occurs because high argon flow actively cools the battery, suppressing thermal runaway intensity and distorting measured gas concentrations. This imposes a flow-rate ceiling for valid measurement: 1.5 L/s is the appropriate rate. Both findings define operating boundaries—one thermal, one fluidic—beyond which data no longer reflect intrinsic battery thermal runaway behavior.
must_include: 180 °C anomaly linked to valve-opening temperature and full electrolyte-membrane reaction
Non-monotonic CO-temperature relationship; cannot assume higher temperature always yields higher CO
2.0 L/s causes battery cooling artifact, not improved measurement
1.5 L/s identified as appropriate flow rate for valid results
Each finding defines a specific experimental boundary condition
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming the 180 °C effect applies to all battery chemistries or form factors without additional evidence
Claiming 2.0 L/s flow rate suppresses thermal runaway permanently or prevents it
Generalizing the valve-opening temperature threshold beyond the tested Samsung 18650 configuration

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism-level distinction between a reaction-threshold-driven non-monotonic temperature effect and a flow-induced measurement artifact, and their implications for experimental boundary conditions.
analysis.primary_boundary: Both findings are specific to the tested Samsung 18650 at 100% SOC and 200 W heating; neither generalizes unconditionally to other configurations.

solver_view.stem: A cylindrical lithium-ion cell undergoes abuse heating. Circumferential strain-rate crosses 0.1 µε/s at 302 s, venting occurs at 413 s, and thermal runaway is confirmed at 443 s. Surface temperature monitoring is also active. Which statements correctly characterize the relative warning performance of strain-rate versus temperature-based detection under these conditions?
options: A. Strain-rate threshold crossing at 302 s provides more than 140 s of lead time over temperature-based thermal runaway confirmation.
B. Surface temperature signals lead strain-rate signals during early internal heating because convective heat transfer accelerates surface response.
C. Strain-rate detection at 302 s provides more than 100 s of lead time over venting-based alerts triggered at 413 s.
D. Temperature-based confirmation at 443 s and venting-based alerts at 413 s offer equivalent early warning utility because both lag the strain-rate signal.
E. Thermal conduction delay causes surface temperature signals to lag during early internal heating, reducing the responsiveness of temperature-based warning.
F. Strain-rate threshold crossing provides earlier warning than venting only because venting is a lagging indicator of gas pressure, not because strain responds to internal pressure changes.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 443 s minus 302 s equals 141 s, consistent with the stated lead time of more than 140 s over temperature-based confirmation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal conduction delay causes surface temperature to lag, not lead; convective acceleration claim is unsupported and reverses the reported direction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 413 s minus 302 s equals 111 s, consistent with the stated lead time of over 100 s compared to venting-based alerts.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Venting at 413 s provides 30 s more warning than temperature at 443 s; treating them as equivalent ignores this measurable difference.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Thermal conduction delay during early internal heating causes surface temperature lag, directly limiting temperature-based early warning responsiveness.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Strain does respond to internal pressure changes; the causal attribution is partially correct but the exclusionary framing misrepresents the mechanism.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Quantitative lead-time comparison between strain-rate, venting, and temperature-based thermal runaway warning signals
analysis.primary_boundary: Lead-time values are specific to the reported test conditions; generalization to other chemistries or cell formats is not supported
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming temperature always lags strain-rate in all cell formats
Treating venting and temperature confirmation as interchangeable warning tiers

solver_view.stem: Three boundary conditions are tested on a cylindrical cell during thermal runaway: free expansion, biaxial (two-sided) clamping, and full structural confinement. Initial strain-rate threshold crossing times are 302 s, 283 s, and 345 s respectively. Under full confinement, maximum strain levels are significantly lower and growth is delayed. Which of the following statements are INCORRECT explanations of these observations?
options: A. Full confinement delays and reduces external strain because pressure-induced volumetric changes are accommodated as internal compressive stress rather than outward deformation.
B. Biaxial clamping provides the earliest warning because partial constraint amplifies circumferential strain sensitivity relative to free expansion.
C. Full confinement suppresses strain signals entirely, making strain-rate monitoring unreliable for any early warning application regardless of threshold choice.
D. The later threshold crossing under full confinement compared to free expansion occurs because radial expansion is restricted, reducing the rate of external deformation.
E. Free expansion yields earlier warning than full confinement because without constraint, internal pressure directly drives outward casing deformation.
F. Biaxial clamping triggers earlier warning than free expansion because structural symmetry eliminates all measurement noise from the strain signal.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: This accurately restates the supported mechanism: full confinement redirects volumetric changes to compressive stress accumulation internally.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Biaxial clamping does yield earliest warning at 283 s; partial constraint amplifying sensitivity is a mechanistically consistent explanation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (target)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows delayed and lower strain under full confinement, not complete suppression; universalizing to unreliability is an unsupported overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Radial expansion restriction under full confinement directly explains the delayed and reduced external strain-rate threshold crossing at 345 s.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Free expansion at 302 s is earlier than full confinement at 345 s; unconstrained casing deformation driven by internal pressure is the consistent mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (target)
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports noise elimination as the mechanism; attributing earlier warning to signal noise reduction is an unsupported mechanism substitution.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism by which boundary conditions modulate external strain signal amplitude and timing during thermal runaway
analysis.primary_boundary: Observations are specific to the three tested boundary configurations on cylindrical cells; intermediate constraint levels are not characterized
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming full confinement eliminates all strain-based warning utility
Attributing biaxial advantage to noise reduction rather than constraint geometry

solver_view.stem: A coupled thermal-structural model of a cylindrical cell uses one-way coupling: temperature drives reaction kinetics and gas generation, pressure depends solely on gas accumulation, and structural response is driven by pressure. Thermal expansion of the casing is neglected. The model is applied to predict strain during thermal runaway. Which statements correctly identify limitations or valid scope boundaries of this modeling approach?
options: A. Neglecting thermal expansion is more defensible for cylindrical cells than for prismatic cells because electrochemically induced strain dominates in cylindrical geometries.
B. The one-way coupling assumption means structural deformation cannot alter the rate of gas generation or temperature evolution within the model.
C. Because thermal expansion dominates deformation in prismatic cells, this cylindrical-cell model can be directly transferred to prismatic formats without modification.
D. Neglecting feedback from the structural domain to thermal and chemical subsystems is an acknowledged limitation that may affect accuracy if deformation influences heat generation.
E. The model correctly captures all deformation modes in cylindrical cells because pressure-driven strain is the only physically significant contributor to casing deformation.

F. One-way coupling is a valid simplification provided that structural deformation does not significantly alter internal pressure or reaction conditions, a condition the model itself cannot verify internally.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states thermal expansion dominates in prismatic cells and electrochemical strain dominates in cylindrical cells, which is the opposite of this claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: One-way coupling by definition excludes feedback from structural to thermal and chemical domains, so deformation cannot alter gas generation or temperature in the model.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Prismatic and cylindrical cells have different dominant deformation mechanisms; direct model transfer is an unsupported scale and geometry transfer overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The model explicitly acknowledges no feedback from structural to thermal or chemical subsystems, which is a stated limitation with potential accuracy consequences.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Claiming pressure-driven strain is the only significant contributor universalizes beyond what is stated; thermal and electrochemical contributions are acknowledged to exist.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: One-way coupling validity depends on deformation not significantly perturbing pressure or reactions; this condition cannot be confirmed from within a one-way coupled model.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility and scope boundaries of one-way coupled thermal-structural modeling for cylindrical cell thermal runaway
analysis.primary_boundary: Model scope is explicitly limited to cylindrical cells; thermal expansion neglect is justified only for cylindrical geometry based on stated evidence
disallowed_extrapolations: Transferring cylindrical model directly to prismatic formats
Claiming one-way coupling captures all physically significant deformation modes

solver_view.prompt: A cylindrical cell is instrumented with strain gauges measuring circumferential, radial, and axial strain during thermal runaway under free-expansion conditions. Near the geometric centre of the cell, peak strain reaches 13,000 µε, but values drop below 2,000 µε beyond r > 4 mm under fully constrained conditions. Circumferential strain consistently shows the highest amplitude and strain rate, while axial strain shows minimal change. Using these observations, explain: (1) which strain direction is most suitable for early warning sensing and why, (2) what spatial constraint on sensor placement follows from the radial attenuation data, and (3) what modeling limitation must be acknowledged when applying these findings to prismatic cell formats.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Circumferential strain is the most suitable direction for early warning because it consistently exhibits the highest amplitude and strain rate throughout thermal runaway progression, providing the strongest and earliest detectable signal. Axial strain is the least suitable due to minimal changes in both strain and strain rate. For sensor placement, the radial attenuation data indicate that sensors must be positioned close to the cell centre; beyond r > 4 mm under fully constrained conditions, strain drops below 2,000 µε from a peak of 13,000 µε, so sensors placed too far radially outward will significantly underestimate the deformation signal. Regarding model transferability, the thermal-structural model neglects thermal expansion because electrochemically induced strain dominates in cylindrical cells, whereas thermal expansion dominates in prismatic cells; therefore, applying these cylindrical-cell findings or the same modeling assumptions directly to prismatic formats is not supported by the evidence.
must_include: Circumferential strain has highest amplitude and strain rate, making it most suitable for early warning
Axial strain shows minimal change and is least sensitive
Strain drops below 2,000 µε beyond r > 4 mm, constraining sensor placement to near-centre positions
Thermal expansion dominates in prismatic cells while electrochemical strain dominates in cylindrical cells, limiting direct model transfer
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming circumferential strain superiority holds universally across all cell formats without qualification
Asserting that r > 4 mm attenuation boundary applies equally under free-expansion or biaxial clamping conditions
Concluding that the one-way coupled model is invalid rather than acknowledging it as a stated scope limitation

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Integration of strain directionality, spatial attenuation, and model scope boundaries for sensor design and cross-format admissibility judgment
analysis.primary_boundary: Radial attenuation threshold is reported under fully constrained conditions only; model transfer limitation is specific to cylindrical-to-prismatic format change

solver_view.stem: A Samsung ICR18650-26H 18650 cell undergoes external heating. The cell surface temperature is monitored continuously. Which of the following statements correctly describe the thermal sequence and associated gas-release events? Select all that apply.
options: A. The safety valve opens and releases white smoke (pre-runaway gas) when the surface temperature reaches approximately 132°C–167°C.
B. Irreversible internal chemical changes begin once the internal temperature reaches 120°C, preceding safety-valve opening.
C. Dramatic thermal runaway and deflagration occur when the surface temperature reaches 199°C–225°C, with gas venting radially from holes on top.
D. The electrolyte becomes unstable only after the safety valve opens, because valve opening is the trigger for electrolyte decomposition.
E. The safety valve opening and the onset of deflagration occur simultaneously at the same surface temperature threshold.
F. Electrolyte instability begins at 80–100°C, which is below both the safety-valve opening range and the deflagration range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states safety valve opens at 132°C–167°C surface temperature, releasing pre-runaway white smoke.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states irreversible internal changes begin at 120°C internal temperature, which is below the 132°C–167°C valve-opening range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states deflagration and dramatic runaway occur at 199°C–225°C with radial gas venting from top holes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Electrolyte instability starts at 80–100°C, well before valve opening; valve opening does not trigger electrolyte decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows two distinct temperature ranges: 132°C–167°C for valve opening and 199°C–225°C for deflagration; they are not simultaneous.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Electrolyte instability at 80–100°C precedes both valve opening (132°C–167°C) and deflagration (199°C–225°C), consistent with evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Correct ordering and distinctness of thermal thresholds across electrolyte instability, safety-valve opening, and deflagration events
analysis.primary_boundary: Three separate temperature thresholds (80–100°C, 120°C internal, 132–167°C surface, 199–225°C surface) are distinct and sequential, not simultaneous or causally linked in the reversed direction
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating surface and internal temperature thresholds as interchangeable
Inferring valve opening causes electrolyte decomposition

solver_view.stem: An engineer tests vented gas LEL from 18650 LIB thermal runaway across a range of state-of-charge (SOC) values and heating conditions. Which of the following statements about LEL behavior are INCORRECT? Select all that apply.
options: A. LEL increases monotonically with SOC from 0% to 100%, so the highest-SOC cell always presents the highest LEL.
B. At 100% SOC, vented gas has the lowest LEL among tested SOC values, indicating the highest explosion risk.
C. At low SOC, adequate electrolyte-air reaction produces large CO2 quantities, raising the LEL of the gas mixture.
D. At high SOC, incomplete air reaction produces more CO, which raises the LEL because CO is a non-flammable diluent.
E. LEL decreases as heating power increases, with the lowest recorded LEL being 4.80% in this study.
F. LEL increases as heating temperature increases because higher temperature produces a lower proportion of flammable gas.
G. The peak LEL across SOC values occurs at 60% SOC, not at the lowest or highest SOC tested.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LEL first increases then declines with SOC; it is non-monotonic, peaking at 60% SOC, so monotonic increase is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not an error)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states 100% SOC has the lowest LEL among tested values, representing highest explosion risk; this statement is correct, not an error.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not an error)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states low-SOC adequate combustion produces CO2, a non-flammable gas, raising LEL; this is correct.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO is a flammable gas, not a non-flammable diluent; high-SOC CO production lowers LEL, not raises it.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not an error)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states LEL declines with increasing heating power, with lowest LEL of 4.80%; this statement is correct.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states LEL declines (not increases) with higher heating temperature due to greater flammable gas proportion.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not an error)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states the highest LEL occurs at 60% SOC; this statement is correct and not an error.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying incorrect characterizations of LEL dependence on SOC, heating temperature, and heating power, including direction-of-trend and gas-type mechanism errors
analysis.primary_boundary: LEL-SOC relationship is non-monotonic (peak at 60% SOC); LEL decreases with heating temperature and power; CO is flammable (lowers LEL), CO2 is non-flammable (raises LEL)
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending single-cell findings universally to all LIB chemistries
Treating CO as a non-flammable gas

solver_view.stem: During 18650 LIB thermal runaway, vented gas contains CO at concentrations between 2.0% and 9.9%. An engineer uses Le Chatelier's Formula with an inert-gas correction to calculate LEL and compares it to instrument-measured values. Which conclusions are supported by the evidence?
options: A. CO in the vented gas poses a lethal toxic hazard through asphyxia, narcosis, tissue hypoxia, and organ failure, independent of its flammability contribution.
B. The Le Chatelier Formula with inert-gas correction yields LEL estimates within 5% error of instrument-tested values for the samples evaluated.
C. CO concentrations of 2.0%–9.9% in vented gas guarantee ignition upon release because they exceed the CO lower flammability limit of ~12.5%.
D. The calculation method's accuracy below 5% error means it can replace instrument testing for all LIB chemistries without further validation.
E. The maximum CO content of 9.9% in vented gas is sufficient to cause lethal toxic effects even if ignition does not occur.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly lists asphyxia, narcosis, tissue hypoxia, loss of consciousness, organ failure, and death as CO toxic hazards.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence reports errors of 2.1%, 1.9%, and 0.4% for three samples, all below 5%, supporting this conclusion within the tested scope.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO's lower flammability limit is ~12.5%; concentrations of 2.0%–9.9% are below the LFL, so ignition is not guaranteed by CO alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence covers only three samples of one cell type; generalizing to all LIB chemistries is a scale transfer overclaim unsupported by evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states CO causes lethal toxic effects (asphyxia, organ failure); 9.9% CO far exceeds lethal exposure thresholds independent of ignition.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing supported toxic-hazard conclusions from unsupported flammability-ignition overclaims and scope-transfer errors regarding the LEL calculation method
analysis.primary_boundary: CO toxicity evidence applies to the reported concentration range; LEL calculation accuracy applies only to three tested samples of this cell type; CO at 2–9.9% is below its own LFL (~12.5%)
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming CO concentrations below CO's LFL guarantee ignition
Generalizing calculation accuracy to all LIB chemistries

solver_view.prompt: A battery safety engineer is comparing the explosion risk of 18650 LIB vented gas at 40% SOC versus 100% SOC during thermal runaway. She notes that the 40% SOC cell produces a less violent runaway. She concludes that the 40% SOC cell is always safer from an explosion standpoint because less violent runaway means lower flammable gas content. Evaluate her reasoning. Identify the specific mechanism that determines LEL at low versus high SOC, explain why her conclusion may be incomplete, and state which SOC presents the higher explosion risk according to available evidence.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's reasoning is incomplete. At low SOC, less violent runaway allows electrolyte to react adequately with available air, producing large amounts of CO2, a non-flammable gas that dilutes the mixture and raises the LEL—making the gas harder to ignite. At high SOC, the more violent runaway prevents adequate air reaction, producing more CO, a flammable gas, which lowers the LEL. Additionally, 100% SOC yields the lowest LEL among all tested SOC values, representing the highest explosion risk. The non-monotonic LEL-SOC relationship (peaking at 60% SOC) means that 'less violent runaway' does not straightforwardly translate to lower explosion risk; the gas composition mechanism—specifically the CO2 vs. CO balance—governs LEL, not runaway violence alone. Therefore, 100% SOC presents the higher explosion risk.
must_include: Low SOC produces CO2 (non-flammable) via adequate electrolyte-air reaction, raising LEL
High SOC produces CO (flammable) due to inadequate air reaction, lowering LEL
100% SOC has the lowest LEL and highest explosion risk
LEL-SOC relationship is non-monotonic, peaking at 60% SOC
Runaway violence alone does not determine explosion risk; gas composition (CO2 vs CO balance) is the governing mechanism
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming low SOC is always safe from explosion hazard
Asserting the LEL trend generalizes to all LIB chemistries beyond the tested cell
Treating runaway violence as a direct proxy for LEL without accounting for gas composition

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism-level reasoning linking SOC to gas composition (CO2 vs CO) and its effect on LEL, and identification of the non-monotonic LEL-SOC relationship to refute a plausible but incomplete engineering conclusion
analysis.primary_boundary: Evidence is specific to Samsung ICR18650-26H cells; LEL-SOC relationship is non-monotonic with peak at 60% SOC; 100% SOC has lowest LEL; CO2 raises LEL, CO lowers LEL
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim linking violence directly to explosion risk
mechanism substitution confusing runaway severity with gas flammability

solver_view.stem: An 18650 LIB cell is overcharged to 120% SOC. Its self-heating onset temperature (T1) drops to 41 °C compared to 95.8 °C for a fresh cell, while its thermal runaway triggering temperature (T2) remains near 163 °C. A thermal management system maintains cell surface temperature below 60 °C. Which statements correctly characterize the thermal safety implications of this overcharge condition?
options: A. Thermal runaway cannot be initiated within the thermal management system's operating range because T2 is unchanged at ~163 °C.
B. Self-heating can begin within the thermal management system's normal operating range because T1 has dropped to 41 °C, below the 60 °C ceiling.
C. The unchanged T2 implies that separator integrity is unaffected by overcharge-induced side reactions, consistent with its polymer melting point being chemistry-independent.
D. The reduction in T1 is primarily caused by lithium plating, which produces highly reactive metallic lithium that participates in side reactions at lower temperatures than intercalated lithium.
E. Because T2 is unaffected, the overall thermal runaway risk of the overcharged cell is equivalent to that of a fresh cell.
F. The drop in T1 reflects structural damage to the graphite anode crystal lattice caused by overcharge.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T2 unchanged does not prevent thermal runaway risk; T1 falling below the management ceiling means self-heating can start inside normal operating range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T1 = 41 °C is below the 60 °C management ceiling, so self-heating onset falls within the normal operating range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states T2 reflects separator collapse; the separator is a polymer whose melting point is little affected by internal side reactions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly attributes the T1 decrease to lithium plating and the high reactivity of plated metallic lithium at lower temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Unchanged T2 does not equate to equivalent risk; T1 reduction means self-heating initiates far earlier, fundamentally altering the risk profile.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: XRD shows no new peaks or shifts in the overcharged anode, indicating no crystal structure change; T1 drop is attributed to lithium plating reactivity, not lattice damage.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing the differential impact of overcharge on T1 versus T2 and correctly attributing the T1 reduction mechanism
analysis.primary_boundary: T1 reduction is mechanism-specific (lithium plating reactivity) and does not imply T2 change; unchanged T2 does not neutralize the lowered T1 risk
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding equivalent overall risk from unchanged T2 alone
Attributing T1 drop to anode crystal structure damage

solver_view.stem: For 18650 cells overcharged across 105%–120% SOC, experimental data show: (i) interval time Δt between sudden voltage drop and thermal runaway increases ~3.5× from 105% to 120% SOC; (ii) voltage-drop temperature TV decreases by 31 °C over the same range; (iii) internal gas at 120% SOC is dominated by CO2 (62.23%). Which of the following statements about the mechanisms governing Δt and TV are supported by the evidence?
options: A. Accumulated internal gas from side reactions during overcharge increases internal pressure, advancing CID activation and thereby lowering TV.
B. The increase in Δt with overcharge degree occurs because higher overcharge produces more internal gas, which is the primary driver of this trend.
C. CO2 dominance in the gas composition implies that cathode decomposition is the sole source of gas generation during slight overcharge.
D. The decrease in TV with increasing overcharge degree indicates that the separator begins to melt at progressively lower temperatures as overcharge increases.
E. Both the increase in Δt and the decrease in TV share a common causal origin in overcharge-generated gas accumulation.
F. The 3.5× increase in Δt from 105% to 120% SOC means that higher overcharge always provides more warning time before thermal runaway, regardless of operating temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states gas buildup is the major reason for decreased TV by advancing CID activation through internal pressure.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies gas generation from side reactions as the primary reason for the increase in interval time Δt with overcharge degree.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies CO2 dominance but does not attribute it solely to cathode decomposition; claiming a single exclusive source is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TV refers to voltage-drop temperature tied to CID activation, not separator melting; T2 (separator collapse) is nearly unchanged across overcharge degrees.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes both the Δt increase and TV decrease to the same cause: gas generation from overcharge side reactions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Longer Δt does not unconditionally guarantee more warning time; TV decreases with overcharge, so self-heating onset may already be within normal operating range at higher SOC.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying the shared causal mechanism (gas accumulation) behind both Δt increase and TV decrease, while rejecting mechanism substitution and overclaim distractors
analysis.primary_boundary: Gas generation is the identified primary cause for both Δt and TV trends; TV is CID-related, not separator-related
disallowed_extrapolations: Attributing TV decrease to separator melting point change
Claiming CO2 source is exclusively cathode decomposition
Universalizing Δt increase as unconditional safety benefit

solver_view.stem: During slight overcharge of 18650 LIB cells (105%–120% SOC), three degradation modes are tracked: conductivity loss (CL), loss of lithium inventory (LLI), and loss of active material (LAM). Relaxation-voltage dV/dt analysis shows the peak arrival time decreases linearly with overcharge degree. Which statements about degradation onset and lithium inventory scaling are correctly supported?
options: A. CL and LLI are present at all tested overcharge levels (105%–120% SOC), whereas LAM only appears beyond a threshold between 105% and 110% SOC.
B. Lithium plating is the primary mechanism responsible for LLI, and the amount of plated lithium increases with overcharge degree.
C. The linear decrease in dV/dt peak arrival time indicates that reversible lithium content decreases linearly with increasing overcharge degree.
D. Because XRD shows no new peaks or peak shifts in the overcharged anode, neither LLI nor LAM occurs during slight overcharge.
E. LAM onset between 105% and 110% SOC implies that active material loss is the dominant degradation mode at all overcharge levels studied.
F. The linear scaling of reversible lithium loss with overcharge degree implies that capacity fade also scales linearly with overcharge degree across all possible overcharge levels.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states CL and LLI always occur during slight overcharge, while LAM begins only beyond 105–110% SOC threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies lithium plating as the main reason for LLI and states plated lithium amount increases with overcharge degree.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states that linearly decreasing dV/dt peak arrival time indicates linearly decreasing reversible lithium content.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: XRD showing no structural change in the anode does not preclude LLI or LAM; these are electrochemical degradation modes distinct from crystal structure changes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LAM onset threshold does not imply dominance; evidence shows CL and LLI always occur, while LAM appears only above a threshold, making dominance claim unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Linear scaling of reversible lithium loss is established only within the 105–120% SOC range studied; extrapolating to all overcharge levels is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Correctly mapping degradation mode onset thresholds and the linear scaling of lithium inventory loss, while rejecting conflation of structural and electrochemical degradation
analysis.primary_boundary: Linear reversible lithium scaling is established only within 105–120% SOC; XRD structural integrity does not imply absence of electrochemical degradation modes
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring absence of LLI/LAM from unchanged XRD
Extending linear capacity fade scaling beyond tested SOC range
Claiming LAM dominance from onset data alone

solver_view.prompt: An 18650 LIB cell is overcharged to 120% SOC. Compared to a fresh cell, its self-heating onset temperature T1 drops by 54.8 °C to 41 °C, while its thermal runaway triggering temperature T2 remains essentially unchanged at ~161 °C. The interval time Δt between voltage drop and thermal runaway increases ~3.5× relative to a 105% SOC cell, but the voltage-drop temperature TV decreases by 31 °C. Explain, using mechanism-level reasoning, why overcharge simultaneously lowers T1 and increases Δt, and assess whether the longer Δt represents an unambiguous safety improvement for a cell operating under a thermal management system limited to 60 °C.

ground_truth.gold_answer: T1 decreases because overcharge causes lithium plating at the anode. Plated metallic lithium is highly reactive and initiates side reactions with the electrolyte at lower temperatures than lithium intercalated in graphite, lowering the self-heating onset from 95.8 °C to 41 °C. Δt increases because side reactions during overcharge generate gas (dominated by CO2 at ~62%), which accumulates internally and builds pressure that activates the CID at progressively lower temperatures (TV decreases by 31 °C). CID activation disconnects the cell earlier in the heating sequence, extending the time between voltage drop and thermal runaway. However, the longer Δt does not represent an unambiguous safety improvement: T1 at 41 °C already falls below the 60 °C management ceiling, meaning self-heating can initiate within normal operating conditions. The cell may enter a runaway trajectory before any CID-related warning is observable, negating the practical benefit of the extended Δt.
must_include: lithium plating as the mechanism for T1 reduction
high reactivity of plated metallic lithium enabling lower-temperature side reactions
gas generation as the primary cause of both increased Δt and decreased TV
T1 = 41 °C falling below the 60 °C management limit as the reason Δt benefit is not unambiguous
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming T2 decreases with overcharge degree
Attributing T1 decrease to anode crystal structure damage
Concluding that longer Δt always guarantees more usable warning time regardless of operating temperature
Extending findings beyond the 105–120% SOC slight-overcharge regime tested

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Integrating the T1 reduction mechanism (lithium plating reactivity) with the Δt increase mechanism (gas-driven CID activation) and evaluating whether the Δt benefit is negated by the T1 drop relative to the management system ceiling
analysis.primary_boundary: Analysis is bounded to slight overcharge (105–120% SOC) in an 18650 cell; T2 invariance and the 60 °C management ceiling are the critical boundary conditions for the safety assessment
intended_error_modes: Treating unchanged T2 as evidence of unchanged overall risk
Treating longer Δt as unconditional safety improvement without checking T1 against operating limits
Mechanism substitution: attributing T1 drop to structural anode damage rather than lithium plating reactivity

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery pack is subjected to volumetric heating at 107 kW·m⁻³. Simulation results show that among all internal self-reaction heat sources, ISC Joule heat contributes 26.3% and cathode decomposition contributes 11.1%. The separator melts before ISC onset, triggering large-scale internal short circuit. Which of the following statements are supported by this evidence?
options: A. ISC is the dominant internal heat source driving thermal runaway under these simulation conditions.
B. Cathode decomposition is the primary trigger for separator failure in this scenario.
C. Separator melting is a prerequisite for large-scale ISC, which then amplifies heat generation.
D. Because cathode decomposition contributes only 11.1%, it can be excluded from all TR propagation models.
E. The 26.3% ISC contribution establishes ISC as the main factor among internal self-reaction heat sources at this heating power.
F. ISC dominance at 107 kW·m⁻³ implies ISC will always dominate at any heating power level.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Simulation at 107 kW·m⁻³ shows ISC at 26.3% is the largest single internal heat contributor, directly supporting dominance.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Separator failure is caused by temperature reaching its melting point, not specifically by cathode decomposition; mechanism substitution error.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states separator melting causes large-scale ISC, and ISC then releases heat; this two-step causal chain is directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows cathode decomposition contributes 11.1% at one condition; excluding it universally is an unsupported unconditional claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The simulation explicitly concludes ISC is the main factor in self-reaction heat at this heating power, consistent with 26.3% share.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 26.3% finding is specific to 107 kW·m⁻³; extending dominance to all heating powers is an unsupported regime transfer error.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Causal chain from separator melting to ISC dominance and admissibility of that conclusion under stated simulation conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings are specific to 107 kW·m⁻³ heating power simulation; conclusions do not generalize unconditionally
disallowed_extrapolations: ISC dominance at all heating powers
cathode decomposition as separator failure trigger
universal exclusion of cathode decomposition from models

solver_view.stem: A battery pack model is tested across heating powers from 95 to 155 kW·m⁻³. Evidence shows that as heating power increases, TR critical time decreases while TR critical temperature increases. Separately, at 143 kW·m⁻³, TR cannot occur when air convection coefficient exceeds 15 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹. Which statements are consistent with this evidence? Identify all that apply.
options: A. Higher heating power causes the battery to reach the TR threshold faster, reducing critical time.
B. Higher heating power lowers the TR critical temperature because the battery heats more rapidly.
C. At 143 kW·m⁻³, sufficient convective cooling can prevent TR onset entirely.
D. A convection coefficient of 10 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ is sufficient to prevent TR at 143 kW·m⁻³.
E. The inverse relationship between critical time and heating power holds for all volumetric heating rates, including those below 95 kW·m⁻³.
F. Convective cooling effectiveness interacts with heating power to determine whether TR occurs.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states critical time decreases with increasing heating power from 95 to 155 kW·m⁻³, consistent with faster thermal accumulation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows critical temperature increases with heating power, opposite to this claim; trend reversal error.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states TR cannot occur at 143 kW·m⁻³ when convection coefficient exceeds 15 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹, supporting this conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The threshold is >15 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹; 10 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ is below this, so TR prevention is not supported at this coefficient.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The trend is reported only for 95–155 kW·m⁻³; extending it below 95 kW·m⁻³ is an unsupported extrapolation beyond the tested range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The convection threshold for TR prevention is stated at a specific heating power, implying their interaction determines TR outcome.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interaction between heating power and convective cooling in determining TR critical conditions and directional trends
analysis.primary_boundary: Heating power trend valid only for 95–155 kW·m⁻³; convection threshold specific to 143 kW·m⁻³
disallowed_extrapolations: Trend below 95 kW·m⁻³
convection coefficient below threshold preventing TR
critical temperature decreasing with power

solver_view.stem: A battery pack with no thermal insulation between clamps and cells is analyzed for TR propagation. Evidence shows that increasing inter-cell gap shortens the TR critical time of the first battery (Bat1) but lengthens the TR propagation delay between batteries. No insulation is present. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. Without insulation, clamp heat conduction is the dominant inter-battery heat transfer path and must be included in TR propagation models.
B. Increasing gap always reduces TR risk for the entire pack by slowing propagation to adjacent cells.
C. Increasing gap concentrates heating on Bat1, accelerating its TR onset.
D. Increasing gap simultaneously reduces propagation delay and Bat1 critical time, optimizing pack safety.
E. The effect of gap on Bat1 critical time and propagation delay are opposing, creating a design trade-off.
F. Clamp thermal resistance is irrelevant when inter-cell gaps are large.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that without insulation, clamp conduction is the main heat transfer path and must be included in propagation models.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While propagation delay increases, Bat1 critical time decreases; claiming overall risk reduction is an unsupported causal overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states more heat concentrates on Bat1 with increasing gap, shortening its TR critical time.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows gap increase shortens Bat1 critical time and increases propagation delay; these effects are opposite, not both favorable.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Gap increase shortens Bat1 critical time (worse) but lengthens propagation delay (better), constituting an explicit design trade-off.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states clamp conduction dominates when no insulation exists, regardless of gap size; dismissing it is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Trade-off between Bat1 TR onset acceleration and propagation delay extension when inter-cell gap increases, plus clamp conduction dominance
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings apply to the no-insulation configuration; clamp conduction dominance is conditional on absence of insulation layer
disallowed_extrapolations: Gap increase universally reduces pack TR risk
clamp irrelevance at large gaps
simultaneous improvement of both Bat1 onset and propagation delay

solver_view.prompt: A thermal runaway model for a lithium-ion battery pack must represent the sequence of internal events from initial self-heating through large-scale internal short circuit (ISC). Using the evidence that SEI decomposition begins at 80–120°C, ISC is modeled as occurring at 160°C internal temperature, and separator melting causes large-scale ISC, describe the mechanistic sequence linking these three thresholds. Then explain why ISC Joule heat—rather than cathode decomposition—is identified as the primary driver of TR under the simulated heating condition of 107 kW·m⁻³, and state one limitation on generalizing this conclusion.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The sequence proceeds in three stages: (1) SEI decomposition initiates self-heating at 80–120°C, producing heat that raises internal temperature; (2) as temperature climbs to the separator melting point, the separator fails, enabling large-scale ISC; (3) when internal temperature reaches 160°C, ISC occurs and stored electrical energy (~1.50×10⁵ J) is released over ~35 seconds, generating substantial Joule heat. At 107 kW·m⁻³, ISC Joule heat accounts for 26.3% of self-reaction heat versus 11.1% for cathode decomposition, making ISC the dominant internal heat source under this specific condition. This conclusion is limited to the 107 kW·m⁻³ simulation; the relative contributions of ISC and cathode decomposition may differ at other heating powers or under different boundary conditions.
must_include: SEI decomposition at 80–120°C as the onset of self-heating
Separator melting as the trigger for large-scale ISC
160°C as the ISC onset temperature in the model
ISC contributes 26.3% vs. cathode decomposition 11.1% at 107 kW·m⁻³
At least one stated limitation on generalizing the ISC dominance finding
disallowed_extrapolations: ISC is the dominant heat source at all heating powers
Cathode decomposition can be universally excluded from TR models
The 160°C ISC threshold applies to all battery chemistries or configurations without qualification

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-threshold causal chain from SEI decomposition to ISC, and evidence-bounded interpretation of ISC heat dominance
analysis.primary_boundary: ISC dominance conclusion is specific to 107 kW·m⁻³ simulation conditions and must not be generalized unconditionally
